4.Option: The true burden of COVID-19 infections in Malaysia was substantially higher than reported, with an estimated 9.95 million cases—approximately 4.3 times the number of reported cases—indicating significant underestimation due to limited testing and surveillance.
Evidence: A total of 2.31 million cases were reported in Malaysians between 1 March 2020 and 30 December 2021. Incidence trends were quadrimodal with peaks on 26 March 2020 (n = 235), 30 January 2021 (n = 5,728), 29 May 2021 (n = 9,020) and 12 August 2021 (n = 21,668). Cases aged 18 and 50 had the highest daily reported case density between August and September 2021 (Fig 1).

A total of 27,711 deaths were reported between 1 March 2020 and 30 December 2021 (Table 1). Mortality trends were quadrimodal with peaks reported on 29 March 2020 (n = 7), 18 February 2021 (n = 25), 2 June 2021 (n = 126), and 9 August 2021 (n = 360). The age-specific mortality rate is highest among individuals aged above 60 between August and September 2021 (Fig 2). The distribution of cases by age remained similar across time.

A CFR of 1.0 (95% CI: 0.9–1.0) was reported for Malaysia cumulatively across the study period. Cumulative CFR across the study period is 0.01, 0.02, 0.34, 2.47, and 7.79 in individuals aged between 0–11, 12–17, 18–50, 51–65, and more than 65 years, respectively. The lowest non-zero age specific aCFR utilised for the assumed IFR were 0.002, 0.02, 0.07, 0.85, and 4.3 79 in individuals aged between 0–11, 12–17, 18–50, 51–65, and more than 65 years, respectively. This assumed IFR approximated the pooled age-specific IFR from the literature. The aCFR trends are unimodal in individuals aged 0–11 (peak in October 2020) and 12–17 years old (peak in July 2021). The aCFR trends are bimodal in individuals aged 51–65 and above 65, respectively, with peaks in July 2020 and 2021. The aCFR trends are trimodal in individuals aged 18–50, with peaks in January 2020, July 2020, and July 2021. The aCFR is consistently highest in individuals above 65 years old (Fig 3).

A total of 9,955,000-cases (95% CI: 6,626,000–18,985,000) cases were estimated to have occurred in Malaysia, corresponding to a prevalence of 32.8%. The relative percentage of cases in 0–11, 12–17, 18–50, 51–65, and more than 65 years age groups were 19.9% (n = 1,982,000, 95% CI: 687,000–3,393,200), 2.4% (n = 236,000, 95% CI: 177,000–3,130,000), 66.1% (n = 6,577,000, 95% CI: 4,766,000–10,525,000), 9.1% (n = 901,000, 95% CI: 783,000–1,063,000), and 2.6% (n = 256,000, 95% CI: 210,000–332,000), respectively. Prevalence of infections was highest in those aged 18–50 years (43.9%), followed by those aged 0–11 years (31.9%), 51–65 years (21.9%), above 65 years (12.6%), and 12–17 years (7.9%) (Table 2).

Overall time trends of the age-specific monthly incidence rate are similar to reported infections with four major peaks. Underestimation is highest between June and September 2021. Underestimation of infections was highest in those aged 0–11 years (Adjustment Factor, AF = 5.5; 95%CI: 1.9–10.9), followed by those aged 18–50 years (AF = 5.0; 95% CI: 3.6–7.9), 51–65 years (AF = 2.9; 95% CI: 2.5–3.5), above 65 years (AF = 1.8; 95% CI: 1.9–10.9), and 12–17 years (AF = 1.3, 95% CI:1.0–17.6) The largest number of estimated cases in individuals aged in 0–11, 12–17, 18–50, 51–65, and more than 65 years is observed in August 2021 (n = 450,000, 95% CI: 150,000–900,000), August 2021 (n = 60,000, 95% CI: 45,000–900,000), August 2021 (n = 2,166,000, 95% CI: 1,557,000–3,476,000), July 2021 (n = 258,000, 95% CI: 223,000–305,000) and August 2021 (n = 52,000, 95% CI: 42,871–68,366), respectively (Fig 4).
4.Rationale: The passage provides detailed information about the estimated number of cases, which is approximately 4.3 times the number of reported cases. This significant difference between the estimated and reported cases indicates substantial underestimation due to limited testing and surveillance, which is a common issue with high-mutation-rate RNA viruses with airborne transmission and zoonotic potential. The passage does not directly mention the characteristics of the virus, but the context of the study and the methods used to estimate the true burden of disease imply that these factors contribute to the underestimation.

5.Option: The geographical variation in the relationship between climate and murine typhus incidence reflects a consistent pattern where higher temperatures and rainfall uniformly suppress R. typhi transmission across all districts in Taiwan.
Evidence: Univariate negative binomial model.Univariate negative binomial regression showed that two determinants, income and population density, were insignificantly associated with the murine typhus IR (P>0.05). In addition, elevation, daysT30, relative humidity, and distance to operating or abandoned international ports were dropped from further multivariate analyses due to collinearity (rs >0.7) with other more significant variables (Table 1).

Non-spatial multivariate negative binomial model.Six variables (rainfall, temperature, artificial surface, forest, distance to operating ports, and distance to abandoned seaports) were included in the non-spatial multivariate negative binomial regression model. Artificial surface, forest and distance to abandoned seaports were removed from the final non-spatial model as they were not significantly associated with the IR (P>0.05). Of the three remaining variables, temperature was positively associated (per 1°C increase, r = 0.30, P<0.001), while rainfall and distance to operating ports were negatively associated (per 1000 mm increase, r = -0.51, P<0.005; per 10 km increase, r = -0.15, P<0.001, respectively), with IR.

Bayesian negative binomial CAR model.A Bayesian negative binomial CAR model, which corrected for spatial dependency in the residuals, showed that temperature (posterior mean = -0.75 per 1000mm increase, 95% CrI = -1.4 to -0.1), rainfall (mean = -0.24 per 1°C increase, 95% CrI = -0.4 to -0.05) and distance to operating ports (mean = -0.21 per 10km increase, 95% CrI = -0.4 to -0.05) were significantly and negatively associated with murine typhus IR (95% CrI did not cross 0).

Geographically weighted regression model.The adaptive bi-square kernel was selected because it resulted in the lowest AICc value (547.2) compared to the other three kernel types (fixed Gaussian: 562.4, adaptive Gaussian: 604.9, fixed bi-square: 589.4). The statistical significance of temperature, rainfall and distance to operating ports in the spatial distribution of murine typhus cases varied considerably between districts. Temperature was negatively associated with murine typhus IR (pseudo t <-1.96, P<0.05) mainly in central-east, central-west and southwest Taiwan, but there were also a few districts in northwest Taiwan with a positive correlation (pseudo t >1.96; Fig 6A). A negative association between rainfall and murine typhus IR occurred mainly in northeast, central-west and southwest Taiwan; by contrast, some districts in northwest and southwest Taiwan revealed a positive correlation (Fig 6B). Lastly, distance to operating ports was negatively related to murine typhus IR, primarily in districts surrounding Kaohsiung port and to a lesser degree near Taichung port although there were also two districts close to Taichung port that instead exhibited a positive association (Fig 6C).
5.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the relationship between climate and murine typhus incidence is not uniform across all districts in Taiwan. Instead, it shows that the association between temperature, rainfall, and murine typhus incidence varies by district, with some districts showing a negative association and others a positive association. This indicates that the relationship between climate and murine typhus incidence is not consistent across the entire country, and therefore the option is incorrect.

5.Option: After controlling for distance to operating international seaports, murine typhus incidence decreased with increasing rainfall and temperature, suggesting that local climate may influence the inland spread of R. typhi following introduction at ports.

Non-spatial multivariate negative binomial model.Six variables (rainfall, temperature, artificial surface, forest, distance to operating ports, and distance to abandoned seaports) were included in the non-spatial multivariate negative binomial regression model. Artificial surface, forest and distance to abandoned seaports were removed from the final non-spatial model as they were not significantly associated with the IR (P>0.05). Of the three remaining variables, temperature was positively associated (per 1°C increase, r = 0.30, P<0.001), while rainfall and distance to operating ports were negatively associated (per 1000 mm increase, r = -0.51, P<0.005; per 10 km increase, r = -0.15, P<0.001, respectively), with IR.

Geographically weighted regression model.The adaptive bi-square kernel was selected because it resulted in the lowest AICc value (547.2) compared to the other three kernel types (fixed Gaussian: 562.4, adaptive Gaussian: 604.9, fixed bi-square: 589.4). The statistical significance of temperature, rainfall and distance to operating ports in the spatial distribution of murine typhus cases varied considerably between districts. Temperature was negatively associated with murine typhus IR (pseudo t <-1.96, P<0.05) mainly in central-east, central-west and southwest Taiwan, but there were also a few districts in northwest Taiwan with a positive correlation (pseudo t >1.96; Fig 6A). A negative association between rainfall and murine typhus IR occurred mainly in northeast, central-west and southwest Taiwan; by contrast, some districts in northwest and southwest Taiwan revealed a positive correlation (Fig 6B). Lastly, distance to operating ports was negatively related to murine typhus IR, primarily in districts surrounding Kaohsiung port and to a lesser degree near Taichung port although there were also two districts close to Taichung port that instead exhibited a positive association (Fig 6C).

10.Option: The calcification process in neurocysticercosis is dynamic, with peak calcification occurring at 8 months after antiparasitic treatment, followed by a gradual reduction in calcium content and structural changes consistent with fibrotic replacement.
Evidence: Ex vivo CT scan demonstrated the presence of at least one calcified lesion in 13/15 pigs (86.7%) after APT.

The overall proportion of residual calcification after APT was 63.9% (156 calcified lesions on CT scan/ 244 viable cysts on basal MRI). We observed higher proportions of residual calcification in treated NCC pigs at 4 and 8 months (83.3% [50/60] and 82.8% [77/93]) compared with treated NCC pigs at 12 months (31.9% [29/91]).

Histopathological evaluation of calcified granulomas revealed different degrees of inflammatory and fibrotic responses in the perilesional tissue according to post–treatment time points in H&E and MTC stains.

At 4 months after APT, most calcified granulomas showed signs of effacement due to dystrophic mineralization, with only fragments of scolex hooks or a cyst silhouette with a central scolex surrounded by multinucleated cells, lymphocytes, plasma cells, and tortuous band of at immature fibrous connective tissue.

At 8 months after APT, calcified granulomas were partially or completely effaced by dystrophic mineralization, with presence of necrosis and surrounded by a variety of inflammatory cells including lymphocytes, plasma cells, histiocytes, and multinucleated giant cells; signs of neovascularization in the surrounding tissue as well as dense bands of mature fibrous connective tissue with some areas showing whorls and projections into the neutrophil were also observed.

By 12 months after APT, calcified granulomas showed advanced degeneration characterized by extensive mineralization and effacement and were surrounded by varying degrees of inflammatory cell infiltrates including lymphocytes, plasma cells, multinucleated giant cells, whereas the external layer was sequestered by a dense, thick, and disorganized mature fibrous connective tissue.
10.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of a dynamic process of calcification and degeneration in neurocysticercosis, with peak calcification at 4 and 8 months after antiparasitic treatment, followed by a reduction in calcification and structural changes consistent with fibrotic replacement by 12 months. 25.Option: Patients with severe neutropenia due to benzylpenicillin should routinely receive granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) or prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent life-threatening complications.
Evidence: The other 11 patients with mild or moderate neutropenia finished the 14-day therapy with close monitoring of CBC and observation for sequelae of neutropenia. None experienced any severe complication of therapy and all had recovery of a normal ANC within seven days.
Some of these patients received a second round of therapy with benzylpenicillin three months later and either had no neutropenia or experienced similar neutropenia without symptoms.

One patient with syphilitic meningitis, ocular and otic syphilis (case 12) had an itchy rash on the trunk on day 4 and fever (maximum 38.5℃) on day 5. Repeat laboratory testing revealed that his ANC declined to 1.13×109/L. Thus, antihistamine and methylprednisolone (40mg daily) were commenced instead of benzylpenicillin. His symptoms and CBC count normalized on day 8. Subsequently, an alternative regimen of intravenous ceftriaxone (1.0 g every 12 hours for 15 days) (13) was reinstituted uneventfully three months later.

Another two patients (case 1 with general paresis and case 13 with syphilitic meningitis) did well until they had fever, and repeat CBC revealed thrombocytopenia and severe neutropenia (ANC of case 1: 0.04×109/L; ANC of case 13: 0.21×109/L) near the end of therapy. Benzylpenicillin was discontinued. Despite mild symptoms, their fever in the context of a severe neutropenia caused a high level of concern for underlying life-threatening infection, and both patients were transferred to the emergency department. The results of bone marrow examination, Coombs' test, and cytomegalovirus and rubella virus IgM antibodies were not significant. They were given symptomatic relief and supportive treatment rather than human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, glucocorticosteroid or other prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics. Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin. Initiation of intravenous ceftriaxone did not induce neutropenia three months later in either patient.
25.Rationale: The passage indicates that patients with severe neutropenia did not experience severe complications and recovered with supportive treatment and discontinuation of benzylpenicillin. Therefore, the statement that such patients should routinely receive G-CSF or broad-spectrum antibiotics is not supported by the study findings.

Thus analyzed, Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a substantial increase in time to first diarrhea episode with ≥3 watery stools/24 h (p<0.001), but only so among children without multi-nutrients, whereas no association was found between Giardia infection and diarrhea in those receiving multi-nutrients.

29.Option: The observed reduction in diarrhea incidence among Giardia-infected children is likely due to improved sanitation and health-care seeking behavior in this population.
Evidence: The study profile is shown in Figure 1. G. intestinalis was detected in 192 children (31%). We failed to obtain fresh stool samples for 54 children at baseline and for 50 children during the second survey, when 20 children (3%) were lost to follow-up (3 died; 2 were withdrawn by parents; 15 emigrated from the study area). Baseline characteristics are presented in Table 1. Children with Giardia infection were on average 3.3 months older than their uninfected peers, resided somewhat closer to the dispensary, had a lower prevalence of inflammation, marginally higher hemoglobin concentrations, as well as marginally lower plasma concentrations of soluble transferrin receptor and folate. All other biochemical indicators of nutritional status were similar, and we found no evidence that Giardia was associated with symptoms as reported by the mother (Table S2).

Adjustment for age and distance to the dispensary led to smaller but still substantial associations between baseline Giardia infection and diarrhea (all events), whilst interaction effects between infection and the multi-nutrient intervention remained virtually unchanged (Figure 4). Further adjustment for baseline factors previously found to be prognostic for diarrhea (height-for-age z-scores, sex, inflammation and use of mosquito nets) led to similar effect estimates (not shown).

We also explored the association between Giardia infection and diarrhea within age classes in children without multi-nutrients; although the numbers of cases within these strata was low, all estimates pointed towards a protective association (HR: 0.36 [0.13 to 1.01], 0.81 [0.43 to 1.55], 0.19 [0.04 to 0.84] in children aged 6–17 months, 18–35 months and 36–60 months, respectively). Also, when restricting the analysis to children who were infected at both surveys (n=98) and those who were never infected (n=294), we observed very similar patterns and came to the same conclusions (not shown).

Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the claim that the observed reduction in diarrhea incidence among Giardia-infected children is due to improved sanitation and health-care seeking behavior. The evidence provided focuses on the association between Giardia infection and diarrhea, and the impact of multi-nutrient supplementation, without mentioning sanitation or health-care seeking behavior as factors.

The seroprevalence of S. stercoralis infections was 32.9% in Bandamaji and 12.8% in Dole, which revealed a highly significant difference (χ2 = 15.3, p<0.001).

In Dole, hookworm was the predominant species (32.2%). Infections with A. lumbricoides were rare (3.4%), whereas prevalences of 16.8%, 12.7%, and 11.7% were found for T. trichiura, S. stercoralis, and S. haematobium, respectively.
30.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of significant differences in seroprevalence between Bandamaji and Dole, with higher seroprevalence of A. lumbricoides and S. haematobium in Bandamaji and a higher prevalence of hookworm in Dole. This suggests that the GPELF program, which included the distribution of ivermectin and albendazole, may have been more effective in Dole than in Bandamaji.

Infections with A. lumbricoides were rare (3.4%), whereas prevalences of 16.8%, 12.7%, and 11.7% were found for T. trichiura, S. stercoralis, and S. haematobium, respectively.
The overall prevalence of infection with any helminth species, according to different tests examined under a microscope was 73.7% in Bandamaji and 48.9% in Dole.
The prevalence of anemia was 64.7% in Dole and 50.9% in Bandamaji, with 8.1% (16/198) individuals showing moderate-to-severe anemia, and 2.5% (5/198) individuals being severely anemic.
30.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high prevalence of helminth infections and anemia in the studied communities, despite the implementation of control programs. This suggests that the 'worm-problem' and anemia in Zanzibar remain a formidable challenge and cannot be overcome by preventive chemotherapy alone.

30.Index: The age-related decline in S. haematobium infection prevalence suggests that the current control strategies have successfully interrupted transmission in older age groups.
30.Option: The age-related decline in S. haematobium infection prevalence suggests that the current control strategies have successfully interrupted transmission in older age groups.
Evidence: In both settings, the prevalence of A. lumbricoides and T. trichiura decreased with age. Whilst a decrease with age was also observed for hookworm and S. stercoralis in Bandamaji, the age-prevalence curve for these two helminths was relatively stable in Dole. Regardless of the study setting, S. haematobium infections were most prevalent in the 12–14-year-old age group (17.1% in Bandamaji and 26.3% in Dole). No S. haematobium infections were found in the elderly (≥60 years).
In Dole, an incremental increase of age by 1 year was associated with a lower risk of a S. haematobium infection (OR = 0.93, 95% CI 0.90–0.95).
30.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of a decline in S. haematobium infection prevalence with age, particularly in Dole. This suggests that the current control strategies may have successfully interrupted transmission in older age groups.

33.Option: Patient-reported preferences for oral and topical formulations are primarily driven by aesthetic concerns related to scarring and social stigma, rather than by treatment convenience or burden.
Evidence: In general, patients preferred oral and topical treatments due to the easier administration and being perceived as ‘fitting’ the disease.

This conceptually interesting discussion was mentioned by different patients, who expressed that, since the infection is local, they would prefer the treatment to be also applied locally: a topical treatment for a local infection.
Most Colombian patients, both from the military and the civilian population, had a preference for ointments over injections.
One mentioned the potential timeliness of applying a cream to the wound, assuming than an early treatment may limit the infection.
33.Rationale: The passage indicates that patients' preferences for oral and topical treatments are driven by the ease of administration, the perceived fit of the disease, and the desire for local application of the treatment. While scarring and social stigma may be concerns, the passage does not suggest that these are the primary drivers for the preference. Instead, the focus is on the convenience and the nature of the disease, which is local and often visible on the skin. Therefore, the option is incorrect as it misrepresents the primary reasons for patient preferences.

33.Option: Incorporating patients' preferences regarding treatment duration, formulation, and cost into Target Product Profiles (TPPs) for cutaneous leishmaniasis can improve the design of new treatments and enhance their real-world effectiveness and accessibility.
Evidence: Patients described their experience with the types, timing and sequence of tests required to monitor the treatment safety.
Patients’ descriptions of multiple adverse events in response to pentavalent antimony, pentamidine and miltefosine are in line with the literature.
Patients often saw AEs as an inevitable part of the treatment, and accepted them.
Fears of receiving treatment due to the injections and the side effects were illustrated by two Colombian soldiers.
In contrast, three Brazilian patients described their positive experiences with intralesional administration of antimonials.
Trade-offs made are reflected in how patients chose to ‘ignore’ the risks associated to the treatment when weighted against the need of being cured.
Treatment regimen was found to be a TPP category rich in themes and trade-offs.
Patients perceived long treatment durations as burdensome, particularly against the need to balance work obligations, and in connection with indirect costs (days of work lost).
Twenty days to one month were seen as acceptable by some patients, although they prefer the faster option to heal the skin lesion.
The theme of compliance was central. In general, being treated, and taking the full treatment course, was seen as important to avoid relapse and sequelae.
Many respondents continued treatment despite side effects. Reasons for this were the fear of disease progression and its consequences.
Other patients reported treatment being paused, or suspended, due to adverse events, thus prolonging the overall treatment duration.
Regarding treatment frequency, daily administration of parenteral drugs was considered as too frequent, and patients preferred weekly injections.
The availability of the drug or trained healthcare providers also influenced the decisions of where to receive treatment.
Costs were frequently mentioned by patients, especially those related to procedures such as administration of injections, consultations, diagnostic tests, and transport.
Indirect costs were mainly related to days of work lost and were mentioned as significant.

Taking time off during treatment without payment was considered difficult for patients who worked as day laborers, or self-employed, some were not able to work at all during treatment, or not able to find work.
Having to stay away from home for treatment was another reason for indirect costs and often affected patients’ income prospects.
In addition, patients emphasized the need for investments in research, to develop alternative drugs with a better safety profile.
In addition, some patients emphasized the need to invest in vaccine development, knowing the risk of reinfection for people living in endemic areas.

Incorporating patients' preferences regarding treatment duration, formulation, and cost into Target Product Profiles (TPPs) for cutaneous leishmaniasis can improve the design of new treatments and enhance their real-world effectiveness and accessibility.
33.Rationale: The passage provides detailed insights into patients' experiences and preferences regarding various aspects of treatment, including safety, tolerability, efficacy, formulation, treatment regimen, and cost. It also highlights the importance of considering these preferences in the development of new treatments to ensure they are effective and accessible in real-world settings. The option is correct as it is directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

One Austrian patient describes paying a 140 EUR (154 USD) fee for specialized care to shorten waiting times, while a Brazilian patient described the challenges trying to access specialized care in their town, both due to the costs of the appointment and limited of availability of specialists.

Some patients, particularly among those living in remote areas, reported significant transport costs to treatment facilities using a variety of means of transport, as well as time lost due to the journey.

This aspect is closely related to Geographical barriers (S2 File).

Taking time off during treatment without payment was considered difficult for patients who worked as day laborers, or self-employed, some were not able to work at all during treatment, or not able to find work.
Having to stay away from home for treatment was another reason for indirect costs and often affected patients’ income prospects.
This contrasted with patients with formal employment, such as the Colombian soldiers or the Austrian patients.

In addition, patients emphasized the need for investments in research, to develop alternative drugs with a better safety profile.

33.Option: The study demonstrates that discrete-choice experiments (DCEs) are the most effective method for capturing patient preferences in NTD drug development, surpassing qualitative interviews.
Evidence: We present a methodological and analytical approach as to involve patients in the identification of preferred characteristics of a new drug, and how this could inform TPPs.
We focussed on NTDs, taking cutaneous leishmaniasis (CL) as an exemplar.

We report the methods and findings of a qualitative study assessing preferences for treatment of CL patients from Brazil, Colombia, and Austria, who were infected with New-World Leishmania species and received different types of treatment.
This study is following an exploratory approach, that is, not restricted to pre-defined categories, or patients’ roles as end users.

The coding framework is based on the TPP developed by DNDi specifically for CL treatment [32]. TPP categories are in bold; existing ones were amended by two added during the coding process (denoted with an asterisk *).

Themes, concepts or propositions that describe, help to interpret and explain aspects of the data [31] were articulated and developed by comparison between and within interviews by two researchers (MC and ACE).
Themes were then assessed for potential incorporation into a TPP, and for being considered during drug development in general.

In line with the exploratory nature of the study, we did not want to prioritize; therefore, we did not count the number of times a specific aspect was mentioned (frequency analysis) and did not present themes within a TPP category in a particular order.
33.Rationale: The passage does not mention discrete-choice experiments (DCEs) as a method for capturing patient preferences. Instead, it describes a qualitative study that used semi-structured interviews and a framework approach for data analysis. The passage does not compare the effectiveness of DCEs to qualitative interviews, so the option is incorrect as it introduces information not present in the passage.

34.Option: Assay duration and the proportion of ring stage parasites in the initial sample are critical factors that influence the accuracy of chloroquine susceptibility testing in P. vivax, and insufficient exposure of ring stage parasites to the drug can lead to artificially inflated EC50 values.

35.Option: Although the over-expression of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is often associated with gene copy number variations, regulatory variations in upstream regions of these genes also contribute to their up-regulation, as evidenced by allele frequency correlations with transcription levels and predicted effects on transcription factor binding.
Evidence: Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance.
Among them, seven showed a significant positive correlation between their transcription level and their CNV.
The sequencing of 1 Kb upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations located upstream detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations.
Among them, 23 variations showed a significant correlation between their allele frequency and the transcription level of the downstream gene.
These 23 variations were located upstream of two GSTs and six P450s.

All of them, except those detected upstream CYP9J26, were negatively correlated with transcription levels meaning that the increased frequency of the allele from the reference genome was correlated to the over-transcription of the downstream gene and reciprocally.
Seven variations were potentially favoring the binding of transcription factors previously identified as enhancers of detoxification genes in resistant populations.
35.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that both gene copy number variations (CNVs) and regulatory variations in upstream regions contribute to the over-expression of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations. The evidence includes significant positive correlations between transcription levels and CNVs, as well as the identification of upstream variations that potentially affect transcription factor binding, which in turn may enhance the expression of detoxification genes.

36.Option: Children with low S. mansoni infection intensity exhibit a transcriptional profile that is intermediate between uninfected and high-intensity infected individuals, suggesting a dose-dependent response.

This inference is consistent with the concept of a dose-dependent response in host transcriptional changes in response to varying levels of infection.

In the placentas of Ld-infected dams, the density of the LZ was lower than that of naive controls, and trophoblast cell atrophy and vasodilation were observed (Fig 4C).

Then, cellular infiltration of the placental LZ was evaluated by immunostaining. MΦs and NK cells are known to be the main populations of blood cells in the normal placenta [36]. The placental MΦ population includes CD206+ fetal MΦs, also called Hofbauer cells, and MRP14+ maternal MΦs [37]. Additionally, the placental NK cell population includes uterine NK cells (uNK) and peripheral blood-derived conventional NK cells (cNK), both of maternal origin. The two NK populations can be distinguished by double staining of DBA lectin staining and PAS staining: uNK is DBA+ PAS+, cNK is DBA- PAS+ [38]. Therefore, quantitative analysis of placental infiltrating cells was performed by immunostaining for CD206, MRP14, B220 (B cell marker), and CD3 (T cell marker) and double staining with DBA/PAS. The results revealed that CD206+ MΦ and MRP14+ MΦ decreased, while CD3+ T cells increased in the placental LZ of the Ld-infected group (Fig 5A). The number of B220+ B cells was not affected by Ld infection. CD3+ T cells in the placentas of the Ld-infected group showed focal infiltrates, which corresponded to areas with high expression of adhesion molecules ICAM1 and VCAM1 in serial sections (Fig 5B), suggesting that the adhesion molecules are involved in CD3+ T cell infiltration. Regarding NK cells, while there was no change in the number of cNK cells, the number of uNK cells tended to increase in the placental LZ of the Ld-infected group (Fig 5C). The results showed that Ld infection induced placental villitis with T cell infiltration.

Based on these results, we performed immunostaining for the Leishmania antigen KMP11, and KMP11+ cells were detected in the fetal liver (Fig 4B). KMP11+ cells were detected in the fetal livers of Ld-infected mice (4/11 mice), with the cell numbers ranging from 1 to 2 per section. This is the first report to histologically demonstrate Ld vertical transmission in a VL mouse model. On the other hand, KMP11+ cells were infrequently detected in the placentas of Ld-infected mice, observed in 3 out of 21 samples with 1–3 cells per section. The result demonstrated vertical transmission in our VL mouse model.

Placental degeneration as described above was observed in all placentas of Ld-infected mice regardless of the presence or absence of vertical transmission, and no correlation was observed between the number of infiltrating cells and vertical transmission, although CD206+ MΦ and uNK cells tended to increase slightly in fetuses with vertical transmission (S1 Fig). The results suggest that Ld infection weakens the placental barrier by inducing placental degeneration, and the Ld parasites that are able to pass through the barrier may contribute to vertical transmission.

Finally, RNA-Seq analysis of the placenta was performed to extract molecules related to placental immunopathology in Ld-infected mice. The results identified 82 differentially expressed genes in the placentas of Ld-infected mice compared to naïve controls (S1 Table). Additionally, 61 genes thought to be involved in placental pathology were extracted based on gene function (Table 3). Among them, 29 genes involved in IFN signaling accounted for the most. The identified IFN signal-related genes include genes involved in the induction of IFN (increase in Parp9 [39,40], Nod2 [41,42] and decrease in Ddah2 [43]), genes involved in IFN signal transduction (increase in Stat1 [44], Stat2 [44], Irf1 [45,46]), and genes whose expression is induced by IFN (IFN-stimulated genes, ISGs; increase in Apol11b [47], Iigp1c [48], Zbp1 [49], Iigp1 [48], Igtp [50], Ifi44l [44,46], Ifit1bl2 [44,46], Gbp2b [51], Irgm1 [52], Gbp2 [48], Gbp3 [48], Irgm2 [53], Ifit2 [44,46], Dtx3l [54], Rtp4 [55], Trim21 [56], Oasl2 [44,46], Uba7 [57], Parp10 [58], Samhd1 [59], Oas1b [44,46]). Among the ISGs, Iigp1c, Iigp1, Igtp, Gbp2b, Irgm1, Gbp2, Gbp3 and Irgm2 were IFN-induced GTPases. In addition, a decrease in Fcrl6, whose expression induces IFN-γ promotion, was also observed [60]. The second most abundant gene group included 18 genes involved in MHC I antigen recognition induced downstream of IFN signals [61]. In brief, Ld infection induced the increase in Cd3e (which is involved in TCR-mediated signal transduction from MHC [62]), Tap1 (which mediates the presentation of degraded peptide fragments from the endoplasmic reticulum to MHC I [63]), Nlrc5 (a transactivator of MHC I [64]), Psmb9 (which is involved in the antigen degradation pathway [65,66]), and MHC I components (H2-Q5, H2-Q6, H2-Q7, H2-Q10, B2m, H2-Q2, H2-T22, H2-D1, H2-K2, H2-T26, H2-T7, H2-Q1, H2-Q4 and H2-K1).

On the other hand, in the placentas of the Ld-infected group, the increase in genes involved in cellular immune suppression and inflammation control was also observed. Genes involved in cellular immunosuppression include the increase in PD-L1 (which is involved in suppressing T cell effector responses [67]), the decrease in granzyme and perforin (Gzme, Gzmc, Gzmd, Gzmb, Gzmg, Gzmf and Prf1), and the decrease in Zbtb32 (a negative regulator of the transcriptional regulator GATA3 [68], which acts on Th2 induction and Th1 suppression [76]). Genes related to inflammation control included the increase in Psg16 and Pla2g7 (which induce activation of anti-inflammatory MΦ [69,70]), and the increase in Trip10 (which is important for VCAM1/ICAM1-mediated T cell adhesion and infiltration [71], and also has an inhibitory effect on IFN-γ signaling [72]). In addition, Ctsg, which induces the activation of T cells and NK cells [73,74], was decreased in the placentas of the Ld-infected group. Furthermore, the decrease in Pdgfb, a growth factor important for the development of the placental labyrinth layer [75], was also observed. The results indicate that Ld infection induces the activation of IFN signals simultaneously with cellular immune suppression and inflammation control in the placentas.
43.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that Leishmania infection during pregnancy leads to placental degeneration and increased T cell infiltration, which weakens the placental barrier. This is associated with the activation of IFN signals and cellular immune suppression, as indicated by the changes in gene expression related to IFN signaling and immune regulation. These changes in the placenta are linked to the vertical transmission of Leishmania parasites to the fetus, as evidenced by the detection of Leishmania DNA in fetal tissues and the presence of infected fetal cells. The evidence supports the conclusion that the activation of IFN signals and the resulting cellular immunosuppression and placental degeneration contribute to the vertical transmission of the parasite.

The high mortality rate in immunosuppressed hamsters and the prevention of mortality with passive immunization suggest that the immune system's overactivation plays a central role in disease development. This inference is supported by the evidence that immunosuppression leads to disease and that neutralizing antibodies can prevent disease, indicating the immune system's involvement in disease progression.

73.Option: The passive immunization experiment confirms that the immune system is responsible for the observed disease pathology, as neutralizing antibodies prevented mortality in infected hamsters.
Evidence: Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.

Two groups, BAYV and BCCV, were both immunized with respectively 10,000 units of BAYV- or BCCV-antibodies 1 day prior to infection with 2,000 PFU of BAYV or BCCV. Two control groups, BAYV untreated and BCCV untreated, were not immunized but only infected with 2,000 PFU of BAYV or BCCV. The immunized groups were all alive at the end of the experiment on day 35 post infection.
73.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that passive immunization with neutralizing antibodies from convalescent mice prevented mortality in hamsters infected with hantaviruses. This suggests that the immune system, specifically the antibodies, is responsible for the observed disease pathology, as the presence of these antibodies in the hamsters prevented the disease from progressing to death.

73.Option: The fact that immunosuppressive agents are able to make Syrian hamsters vulnerable for hantaviruses other than Andes virus indicates that an interesting and probably complicated interplay between the virus and the immune system is responsible for the occurrence of hantavirus disease and its associated mortality.
Evidence: Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
The use of immunosuppressive agents was tested by Brocato et al. with the goal of adapting the model for Sin Nombre virus (SNV).

Three groups of 20 female golden Syrian hamsters were challenged with BCCV, BAYV and sterile phosphate-buffered saline. Hamsters were treated with DEX/CYP as described in Table 1. In the groups challenged with BCCV and BAYV all animals died within 21 days after infection. The animals from the control group challenged with sterile phosphate-buffered saline all survived the experiment and were still alive 35 days post infection.
73.Rationale: The passage indicates that immunosuppressive agents made Syrian hamsters vulnerable to hantaviruses other than Andes virus, leading to high mortality. This suggests a complex interplay between the virus and the immune system, as the suppression of the immune system led to increased susceptibility to infection and disease. The fact that immunosuppression can induce disease in hamsters infected with various New World hantaviruses implies that the immune system's interaction with the virus is a significant factor in the occurrence and severity of hantavirus disease.

73.Option: The lack of mortality in immunocompetent Syrian hamsters infected with New World hantaviruses other than Andes virus suggests that viral pathogenicity is primarily determined by host-specific immune responses rather than viral genetic factors.
Evidence: Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with BAYV and BCCV mimics HPS
Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
73.Rationale: The passage does not provide information about the mortality rates in immunocompetent Syrian hamsters infected with New World hantaviruses other than Andes virus. Therefore, it is not possible to infer that viral pathogenicity is primarily determined by host-specific immune responses rather than viral genetic factors based on the given information. The passage only discusses the effects of immunosuppression on disease outcome, not the natural immune response in immunocompetent hosts.

73.Option: The use of immunosuppressive agents enables the induction of lethal disease in Syrian hamsters infected with multiple New World hantaviruses, indicating that the immune system plays a critical role in modulating disease severity and that viral pathogenicity is not solely determined by the virus itself.
Evidence: Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
The use of immunosuppressive agents was tested by Brocato et al. with the goal of adapting the model for Sin Nombre virus (SNV).

Three groups of 20 female golden Syrian hamsters were challenged with BCCV, BAYV and sterile phosphate-buffered saline. Hamsters were treated with DEX/CYP as described in Table 1. In the groups challenged with BCCV and BAYV all animals died within 21 days after infection. The animals from the control group challenged with sterile phosphate-buffered saline all survived the experiment and were still alive 35 days post infection.
Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
73.Rationale: The passage indicates that the use of immunosuppressive agents led to high mortality in hamsters infected with multiple New World hantaviruses, suggesting that the immune system plays a critical role in modulating disease severity. The fact that immunosuppression can induce lethal disease in hamsters infected with various New World hantaviruses implies that the immune system's response is a significant factor in determining the severity of the disease, and that viral pathogenicity is not solely determined by the virus itself.

74.Option: The variation in infection prevalence between villages over time is best explained by differences in treatment coverage and local socioeconomic factors.
Evidence: The mean coverage of antibiotic at a village visit was 94.1% relative to the census, ranging from 73.9% to 100% of those eligible for treatment (Table 1).
In the baseline census, the 16 villages contained 5735 individuals in 1348 households. There were 808 1–5 year-old children, 48.6% of whom were girls (95% CI 45.3 to 51.9%).
In these children, the mean prevalence of clinical activity (defined as either TF or TI in the WHO simplified grading scale), was 91.6% (95% CI 87.8 to 95.5%).
74.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that there is a significant variation in the prevalence of infection between villages, which can be correlated with the coverage of antibiotic treatment and the socioeconomic status of the villages. The high coverage of antibiotic treatment in some villages and the variation in prevalence suggest that these factors play a role in the effectiveness of the treatment. The passage does not directly state that socioeconomic factors are the sole reason for variation, but it is implied that they are a contributing factor, along with treatment coverage.

74.Option: The return of infection after treatment discontinuation suggests that the antibiotic resistance is developing in the chlamydial population.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
The mean prevalence was reduced from 63.5% at baseline (range 26.3% to 85.7%) to 11.5% six months after the first treatment range (0.0 to 24.4%).
The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6% (range 0.0% to 7.0%), a significant decrease from after a single treatment (P = 0.0004).
74.Rationale: The passage does not provide any direct evidence of antibiotic resistance developing in the chlamydial population. The return of infection after treatment discontinuation is noted, but the passage attributes this to the need for repeated treatments rather than to the development of resistance. The observed patterns of infection prevalence over time are explained by the need for repeated treatments rather than by the development of resistance.

74.Option: Infection may never return if it is brought to a low enough level, analogous to the Allee effect found in population biology.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
At 24 months after the last treatment, the mean prevalence of infection was far higher than at 6 months after the last treatment (P = 0.008), but was still only 40% of that at baseline (P<0.0001).
74.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the analogy with the Allee effect, which is a concept from population biology. The return of infection after treatment discontinuation is observed, but the passage does not suggest that infection may never return if it is brought to a low enough level. Instead, it indicates that infection returns after treatments have been discontinued, and the prevalence fluctuates over time.

74.Option: Even after four biannual distributions of azithromycin, infection predictably returns in trachoma-endemic communities, indicating that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without risk of resurgence.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
At 24 months after the last treatment, the mean prevalence of infection was far higher than at 6 months after the last treatment (P = 0.008), but was still only 40% of that at baseline (P<0.0001).
74.Rationale: The passage indicates that even after four biannual distributions of azithromycin, infection predictably returns in trachoma-endemic communities. This suggests that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without risk of resurgence, as the infection levels rise again after the last treatment. The passage provides evidence of the need for continued treatment to maintain low levels of infection and prevent the return of the disease.

76.Option: The higher total costs associated P. vivax infections compared to other Plasmodium species may be explained by longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, and higher caregiver involvement due to the younger age profile of affected patients.
Evidence: P. vivax patients presented with the highest total costs at $199.05 (SD=173.87; Table 4). In contrast to other malaria species, most of the P. vivax costs were direct costs (63%) where hospital and clinic out-of-pocket costs were double the direct costs of malaria due to other Plasmodium species (Table E in S1 Text).
The mean age was 32 years old (SD = 18), and 75% (114/152) were male.
76.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information that directly links the higher total costs of P. vivax infections to longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, or higher caregiver involvement due to the younger age profile of affected patients. The higher costs are attributed to direct costs being double that of other Plasmodium species, but there is no mention of the age profile of patients or the reasons for the increased direct costs. Therefore, this inference cannot be drawn from the passage.

76.Option: The use of standardized wage estimates from national surveys to value productivity losses resulted in a 78% increase in total cost per episode, indicating that the base case underestimates the true economic burden of malaria on households.
Evidence: When using Sabah-specific estimates from the Malaysia Household Income survey (US$223.57 per month) applied to only adults in Scenario 2, the total costs increased by 19% to US$158.85.
When this wage was applied to the time loss of all patients in Scenario 3, the total cost increased to US$178.61, an increase of 37% compared to the base case.
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that using standardized wage estimates from national surveys to value productivity losses resulted in a significant increase in the total cost per malaria episode. This suggests that the base case estimate, which did not use these wage estimates, underestimates the true economic burden of malaria on households in Sabah, Malaysia.

76.Option: Households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to the high prevalence of zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission among working-age males in rural agricultural areas, which increases both direct and indirect costs.
Evidence: P. knowlesi is now the major cause of malaria in Malaysia, with 3000 to 4000 malaria case notifications per year since 2017.
P. knowlesi infections carry a high risk of severe disease, with 6-9% of symptomatic cases presenting to health facilities in East Malaysia classified as severe malaria.
The increase in the incidence of P. knowlesi [22] is primarily in the East Malaysia states of Sabah and Sarawak on the island of Borneo.
The majority of patients (73%, 110/151) had at least mild anemia (WHO age/sex criteria [38]) during their malaria episode, though the anemia status was not known for one patient.
76.Rationale: The passage indicates that P. knowlesi is a major cause of malaria in Malaysia, particularly affecting working-age males in rural agricultural areas. This, combined with the high risk of severe disease and anemia, suggests that households in these areas face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to both direct and indirect costs associated with the disease.

76.Option: Severe malaria cases, all due to P. knowlesi infection, were associated with substantially higher indirect costs, primarily due to longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses among working-age males.
Evidence: The seven severe malaria cases (all P. knowlesi) had 91% higher total costs than non-severe cases (US$239.64 versus US$125.27 respectively, p=0.006) (Table 4), predominantly due to substantially higher indirect costs (US$89.25 mean difference) for severe malaria.

The mean length of time from enrolment to performing the interview was 31 days. Characteristics of the study participants are presented in Table 1. The mean age was 32 years old (SD = 18), and 75% (114/152) were male.
The majority of patients were diagnosed with P. knowlesi infections (71%, 108/152). Seven (5%) patients met the WHO research criteria for severe malaria; all were due to P. knowlesi infection.
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that severe malaria cases, all due to P. knowlesi infection, were associated with substantially higher indirect costs. These higher costs are primarily due to longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses among working-age males, as indicated by the higher total costs and the fact that all severe cases were due to P. knowlesi.

76.Option: Despite the provision of free health-facility-related costs of malaria care by the Ministry of Health, Malaysia, patients in Sabah faced substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131, and a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, particularly when using lower income thresholds.
Evidence: Direct household costs equated to US$53.89 (SD=66.20) per person (41% of the total cost).

The majority of direct costs were related to the hospital admission, although it is unclear whether these costs included direct admission costs or cost associated food and other supplies needed during an admission.
Indirect household costs were the most significant contributor to the total cost burden (58%), with a mean total of US$75.84 (SD=69.67) for each household.

When using the mean monthly self-reported income value of US$148, direct health expenditure for a single malaria episode accounted for 27% of monthly income. Eighty percent (122/152) of patients reported direct health costs above 10% of monthly income, indicating catastrophic health expenditure.

When using a higher defined threshold for catastrophic health expenditure of 25% of monthly income, the direct costs of a malaria episode reported by 38% (57/152) of patients in this survey remained at levels consistent with catastrophic health expenditure.
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that patients in Sabah faced substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131. Additionally, a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, particularly when using lower income thresholds. This indicates that despite the provision of free health-facility-related costs of malaria care by the Ministry of Health, Malaysia, patients in Sabah still faced significant financial burdens.

77.Option: Negative perceptions of tsetse traps are prone to change over time, particularly after exposure to the technology and through community education, leading to sustained acceptance and active participation in control efforts.
Evidence: Participants expressed willingness and appeared motivated to be involved in tsetse control programs; although the HAT+ group expressed this willingness more frequently.

Some participants from the HAT− group, for instance reported that, on some occasion their first contact with traps was accompanied by the explanation of the trap purpose or general explanation that traps are beneficial. This communication mostly happened through personnel carrying out trap deployment or another member of community, who had been informed about it previously. According to participants this information helped them to put aside associations with supernatural powers.

Similarly participants from HAT+ villages, communicated changed negative- initial attitudes: 'Before we heard of them [traps] we didn't like them; but now we have got to know them [the traps], we like them (…). We like them because since that time they deployed them this has reduced the number of tsetse flies in the area'.

This figure indicates that initial acceptance of tsetse traps when they are initiated in the community is low. This was the case HAT+ villages at the beginning of the tsetse control intervention about ten years ago and is currently still evident in HAT− villages. With the time this acceptance increases and is irreversible once achieved.
77.Rationale: The passage indicates that initial negative perceptions of tsetse traps can change over time, especially after community members are educated about their purpose and see the benefits of the traps. This change in attitude leads to sustained acceptance and active participation in control efforts. The evidence shows that community education and exposure to the technology are key factors in changing perceptions, which supports the inference that long-term community responses to interventions are positive and sustained.

77.Option: The geographical proximity of HAT+ and HAT− villages suggests that information about tsetse control programs spreads easily between communities, leading to similar levels of acceptance over time.
Evidence: Villages from HAT+ and HAT− areas lie within 10 kilometres of each other. Villagers from different villages often mix in the local markets and during social events such as celebrations of country independence, women's day, Christmas; hence the differences in views described below were surprising.
77.Rationale: The passage suggests that despite the geographical proximity of HAT+ and HAT− villages, there are still differences in views and perceptions about tsetse control programs. This indicates that information about tsetse control programs does not necessarily spread easily between communities, leading to different levels of acceptance. The evidence does not support the inference that similar levels of acceptance over time are due to the geographical proximity.

77.Option: The effectiveness of tsetse traps in reducing disease incidence is the main reason for community acceptance, regardless of prior exposure or cultural beliefs.
Evidence: The majority of participants described two or three different groups of biting insect, which they classify under category of tsetse (Lugbara, wii or ofii). The subcategories included “the infectious tsetse” and non-“infectious tsetse”, the last being mostly recognized as causing biting nuisance to humans and cattle. The “infectious tsetse” were mostly described as big in size, having sharp mouth parts, lighter in colour compared to other flies, by participants of both groups.

The majority of participants described the purpose of traps for capturing tsetse flies or other insects. In the HAT− group this conclusion was arrived at mostly from observation of trapped insects on in the upper part of the trap. In this group, participants mostly made associations of traps with mosquitoes however some were certain that traps were specifically for killing tsetse flies.

Participants in both groups agreed that traps work effectively, however the HAT+ group provided more specific details. Participants, for instance, used the following observations for describing effectiveness: reduction in tsetse numbers, reduction of tsetse bites, protection of community against tsetse, and reduction of HAT cases in the area.
77.Rationale: While the effectiveness of tsetse traps in reducing disease incidence is mentioned as a reason for community acceptance, the passage also highlights the importance of community education and overcoming initial negative perceptions. The evidence suggests that community acceptance is not solely based on the effectiveness of the traps but also on changing attitudes and perceptions over time. Therefore, the inference that effectiveness is the main reason for acceptance, regardless of prior exposure or cultural beliefs, is not fully supported by the passage.

77.Option: Community willingness to participate in tsetse control is primarily driven by financial incentives, as evidenced by men's expressed interest in government involvement and financial motivation.
Evidence: Participants mostly requested having access to traps and being involved in their deployment and distribution. The HAT+ group reported that they were already contributing to tsetse control by voluntarily maintaining traps, slashing vegetation around watering points, and burning bushes. More women than men reported being involved in these activities. In both groups women expressed more need for empowerment (see a quote below) and requested access to traps more often, while men expressed more need for village decision making, financial motivation and government involvement in tsetse control programs.
77.Rationale: The passage indicates that community willingness to participate in tsetse control is driven by a need for empowerment, access to traps, and involvement in their deployment and distribution. While financial motivation and government involvement are mentioned, the evidence suggests that these are not the primary drivers of community willingness. The need for empowerment and access to traps are emphasized, particularly by women. Therefore, the inference that financial incentives are the primary driver of community willingness is not supported by the passage.

77.Option: The initial fear and suspicion toward tsetse traps in HAT− villages are primarily due to the traps' appearance and color, rather than cultural or supernatural associations.
Evidence: The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action, as one of the participants explained: 'Since it's just a cloth, there would not be much fear… The fear would be: who has placed it there! For what [purpose]'.

The strong emotional reactions expressed by both groups during the first contact with traps were related to the associations of traps with supernatural powers. Participants explained, for instance, that unusual objects, activities or 'suspicious' people moving in their environment made them apprehensive.
77.Rationale: The passage indicates that the initial fear and suspicion toward tsetse traps in HAT− villages are related to their associations with supernatural powers, rather than their appearance and color. The evidence suggests that cultural and supernatural associations play a significant role in shaping community responses to the traps. Therefore, the inference that the initial fear and suspicion are primarily due to the traps' appearance and color is not supported by the passage.

Evidence: This increase was strongly associated with the travel of most village residents to a religious festival in Senegal one month after receiving treatment, which has been previously reported (OR 12.2, p<0.0001, 95% CI 4.0–44.1).
After the 2-month assessment, infection prevalence in these two villages declined progressively to zero at five years.

This conclusion is directly supported by the evidence provided in the passage.

Evidence: The passage does not provide any evidence that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence, independent of antibiotic treatment.
78.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence, independent of antibiotic treatment. Therefore, this conclusion cannot be derived from the passage body.

78.Option: Applying the current WHO intervention algorithms in low prevalence trachoma settings may lead to unnecessary rounds of mass drug administration in uninfected communities.
Evidence: The WHO also published specific Ultimate Intervention Goals (UIG); targets that need to be reached for the national certification of elimination of blinding trachoma.
Where TF%1–9 exceeds 10%, three annual MDAs followed by re-assessment, to determine whether to continue treating, is recommended [8].

This strategy increases the chance that infected persons are treated. However, in low prevalence settings where infection and disease are poorly correlated it may not represent the most efficient way to eliminate trachoma.
78.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the WHO recommends mass drug administration (MDA) when the prevalence of follicular trachoma in children aged 1–9 years exceeds 10%. However, in low prevalence settings where infection and disease are poorly correlated, this may not represent the most efficient way to eliminate trachoma. This suggests that applying the current WHO intervention algorithms in low prevalence trachoma settings may lead to unnecessary rounds of MDA in uninfected communities. This conclusion is directly supported by the evidence provided in the passage.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized care stems from the belief that outpatient treatment is more effective in healing BU lesions than hospital-based care.

80.Option: The model's conclusion that full access to antivenom is cost-saving is contingent on all snakebite victims in ASEAN countries switching from traditional to conventional treatment, which may not be feasible in remote rural areas.
Evidence: In the full access scenario, all snakebite victims who firstly sought traditional healers when access is now full are assumed to switch to conventional treatment.
80.Rationale: The passage does not state that the model's conclusion of cost-saving is contingent on all snakebite victims switching from traditional to conventional treatment. It only assumes that victims who initially sought traditional healers would switch to conventional treatment in the full access scenario. The feasibility of this switch in remote rural areas is not discussed in the passage, making this inference incorrect.

80.Option: Improving access to antivenom in ASEAN countries would be cost-effective only if antivenom effectiveness exceeds a relative risk of death of 1.19 in Myanmar and 0.35 in Lao PDR.
Evidence: Threshold sensitivity analyses were performed to estimate the lowest level of antivenom effectiveness and the highest level of costs of antivenom treatment that would result in the 'not cost-saving' situation where ICER is equal to zero but still considered cost-effective.
80.Rationale: The passage does not make a claim that improving access to antivenom would be cost-effective only if antivenom effectiveness exceeds a relative risk of death of 1.19 in Myanmar and 0.35 in Lao PDR. It only states that these are the levels of effectiveness that would result in an ICER of zero, which is the threshold for cost-effectiveness. The passage does not state that effectiveness must exceed these levels for the intervention to be cost-effective.

80.Option: The high burden of snakebite in ASEAN countries, including 243,000 victims and 16,000 deaths annually, underscores the urgent need for increased antivenom production to meet demand.
80.Rationale: The passage does not discuss the need for increased antivenom production. It focuses on the economic and clinical impact of improving access to antivenom, not on the production of antivenom itself. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

80.Option: The inclusion of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in the model significantly increased the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) per DALY averted, making the intervention less cost-effective.
Evidence: Scenario analyses were performed by incorporating post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in the model as a mental disability occurring in 8% of victims who survived snakebite envenoming [5,36–38]. We performed scenario analyses by excluding indirect costs from the model. Lastly, scenario analyses were performed by increasing the logistic costs from 5% to 10% of antivenom price because these could be higher, especially in archipelagic countries like Indonesia and Philippines.
80.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about the impact of including PTSD in the model on the ICER per DALY averted. It only mentions that scenario analyses were performed with PTSD included, but does not state that it significantly increased the ICER or made the intervention less cost-effective. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

80.Option: Improving access to snake antivenom from the current level to full access in ASEAN countries would result in cost-saving despite higher direct costs, due to substantial reductions in indirect costs from avoided productivity losses.
Evidence: Full access to antivenom had higher direct costs (2 million USD in Philippines to 50 million USD in Indonesia) but less indirect costs (-1,091 million USD in Indonesia to -28 million USD in Philippines) when compared to current access which resulted in total cost savings of 27 million USD in Philippines to 1,040 million USD in Indonesia.

In total, when compared to current access, full access to snake antivenom in ASEAN countries resulted in 9,362 deaths averted (-59%), 230,075 DALYs averted (-59%), and cost savings of 1.3 billion USD (-53%).
80.Rationale: The passage directly states that full access to antivenom results in higher direct costs but also leads to substantial reductions in indirect costs, which, when combined with the savings from avoided deaths and DALYs, results in overall cost savings. This supports the inference that improving access to antivenom would be cost-saving despite higher direct costs.

81.Option: The FP assay's ability to detect compound binding to Brugia Hsp90 is compromised by high background fluorescence from nematode extracts, which necessitates the use of complex normalization procedures in drug screening.
Evidence: In contrast, cy3B-GA bound the C. elegans extracts with very low affinity (Figure 1C). The signal at each time point was virtually indistinguishable from time 0, indicating that the low increase in mP, observed especially at high extract concentrations, was caused by non-specific binding of the tracer or by autofluorescence from the worm extract.
81.Rationale: The passage indicates that the FP assay can detect specific binding to Brugia Hsp90, as evidenced by the high affinity of cy3B-GA to Brugia Hsp90 and the successful displacement of cy3B-GA by GA. It does not state that the assay's ability is compromised by high background fluorescence, nor does it suggest that complex normalization procedures are required. Analysis of the full genomic DNA sequence of the Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln line revealed an output of polished assembly of 609 contigs with the largest contig being 87kb. BLAST of the hap2 gene (BBOV_III006770) against assembly contigs revealed a single hit at contig 1592.
The area covered by contig 1592 was roughly 1,438,762 to 1,455,743 of chromosome 3, which contained a ~6, 448 bp insertion.

Full sequencing of the genome of the Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln confirmed replacement of the hap2 gene by the 5’ hap2 (935 bp), EF promoter (761 bp), luciferase (1,651 bp), gfp-bsd (1,100 bp), 3’ rap1 (1,288 bp), and 3’ hap2 (656 bp) fragments, present in the transfection plasmid.

118.Option: Peptide OR24 P2 showed promising results with sera of dogs and goats, and may replace salivary gland homogenate or recombinant proteins in surveillance for anti-Phlebotomus orientalis IgG.
Evidence: OR24 P1 showed the closest cut-off value to SGH with canine sera, as well as high correlation coefficient (> 0.75), PPV, specificity (> 0.65) and very high NPV and sensitivity (> 0.9). With sheep sera, all statistical values were very high (> 0.9) except for the low sensitivity (0.25).

OR24 P2 had a high correlation coefficient with the SGH for dog and goat sera. It reached comparable PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity as OR24 P1 for dogs and the highest PPV, NPV, specificity and sensitivity for goats (all above 0.75). Correlation and PPV (< 0.45) were low for sheep sera.

OR65 P1 showed similar statistical values as the previous two peptides for dogs but the specificity was slightly lower (0.5). With goat sera, there was a very high cut-off value and a low correlation coefficient (0.55). The second highest correlation (0.7) was achieved with this peptide and with sheep sera but the PPV and sensitivity (< 0.35) were low.

OR65 P2 was the second best antigen for goats, with the lowest cut-off value, high correlation, PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity (all above 0.65). Although high PPV, NPV, and specificity (> 0.9) were detected with sheep sera, this peptide achieved low correlation and NPV (< 0.6).
118.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that peptide OR24 P2 has high statistical values (correlation coefficient, PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity) when used in ELISA experiments with sera from dogs and goats. The evidence directly supports the inference about the utility of OR24 P2 in monitoring exposure to Phlebotomus orientalis.

119.Option: Malathion selection had a negative effect on resistance to other insecticides, including temephos and deltamethrin, suggesting that resistance mechanisms to malathion may divert metabolic resources away from resistance to other classes of insecticides.
Evidence: Selection with malathion did not induce cross-resistance with temephos, although both are OP (Tables 1 and S3). In contrast, temephos resistance decrease was more noticeable in malathion selected groups than in the control ones–both RR and SR indexes suggested such a trend. In particular, temephos resistance reduction was more pronounced in Crato, the population with the highest initial RR.

When the present study started, diflubenzuron had been recently introduced in the country to control A. aegypti larvae. Resistance indexes for this CSI compares adult emergency inhibition rates (EI), and not lethal concentrations. When compared to the original populations, although some differences in RR and SR for DFB were observed between C and S groups, all values obtained were considered compatible with a susceptible status (Tables 1 and S4).

All samples were considered highly resistant to deltamethrin. Surprisingly, the simple laboratory rearing, in the absence of any insecticide (groups C1, C2), exacerbated PY resistance levels in both populations (Tables 1 and S5). In particular, the SR index confirmed the marked deltamethrin resistance increase in the control groups, mainly in Aracaju (S5 Table). This increase in deltamethrin resistance, however, was not observed in the S1-3 groups, selected with malathion, independently of the analytical method adopted (RR, SR and 95% confidence limits overlapping). In opposition, malathion selected groups, and particularly Crato mosquitoes, exhibited reduction of deltamethrin resistance levels.
119.Rationale: The passage indicates that malathion selection led to a decrease in resistance to other insecticides such as temephos and deltamethrin, which suggests that the resistance mechanisms to malathion may be different from those to other insecticides. This could imply that the metabolic resources or pathways used for malathion resistance might be different, potentially diverting them away from resistance to other classes of insecticides. This is supported by the observed decrease in resistance to temephos and deltamethrin in the populations selected with malathion.

119.Option: The lack of changes in acetylcholinesterase (Ace) activity suggests that malathion resistance in A. aegypti is primarily mediated by metabolic detoxification rather than target-site insensitivity.
Evidence: Total activity of the OP target site, AChE, remained equivalent to the original samples. We also investigated Ace using the WHO susceptibility criterion: inhibition of 70% or more of Ace's activity with propoxur (AChI); in all cases the results are indicative of a susceptible enzyme (Tables 3, 4 and S7). According to this criterion, Ace of both parental Aracaju and Crato populations has a susceptible profile.
119.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the acetylcholinesterase (Ace) activity remained equivalent to the original samples and that the results are indicative of a susceptible enzyme according to the WHO susceptibility criterion. This suggests that malathion resistance in A. aegypti is not primarily mediated by changes in Ace activity, which would be indicative of target-site insensitivity. Instead, the resistance is likely due to other mechanisms, such as metabolic detoxification.

119.Option: The higher metabolic resistance observed in adult mosquitoes compared to larvae suggests that adult-stage selection would be more effective in generating cross-resistance to other insecticides.

In relation to the parental samples, Aracaju adult females tended to present higher MFO and GST activities, the later being revealed by both criteria in control samples (Tables 3 and S7) but detected only after comparison of median values in S ones (S7 Table). For both populations more changes and higher activities were noted in adult mosquitoes, compared to the larval stage, in all C and S groups. The exceptions were α-EST and β-EST activities, which are higher in larvae (S7 Table).
