467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers of New World alphaviruses by interfering with viral assembly and/or budding, likely through sequestering capsid in the nucleus and disrupting capsid/glycoprotein interaction at the plasma membrane.
Evidence: Chemically inhibiting the host’s nuclear import and export proteins alters VEEV capsid localization [18]. Using the same rationale, novel CRM1 inhibitors were tested for their ability to alter capsid localization. Four SINE compounds (S1A Fig) were first screened for toxicity in two separate cell lines, treated for 24 hours, and determined to be non-toxic at 2.5 μM (S2 Fig, [39, 40]). Next, Vero cells treated with non-toxic concentrations of active CRM1 inhibitors (KPT-185, KPT-335, KPT-350), inactive KPT-301, or DMSO were infected with VEEV-TC83, and capsid localization assessed at 16 hpi by confocal microscopy. As seen previously [17, 18], VEEV capsid localized to both the cytoplasm and nucleus in DMSO-treated cells. A similar phenotype was seen upon treatment with the inactive compound, KPT-301. The three active compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350, confined capsid to the nucleus (Fig 1A and S3A Fig), yielding a localization pattern similar to that seen with treatment by the well-documented CRM1 inhibitor, Leptomycin B [18]. As a control, TC83 virus with a mutated capsid that is defective in nuclear import (TC83_Cm; [38]) was analyzed. TC83_Cm infected cells treated with DMSO, Leptomycin B, or KPT-185 all displayed capsid cytoplasmic staining (Fig 1A). To quantitate capsid localization, the nuclear to cytoplasmic fluorescence ratio (Fn/c) was measured as described previously ([18] and S1B Fig). An Fn/c value greater than one denotes predominately nuclear fluorescence, whereas a score below one indicates predominantly cytoplasmic fluorescence. Treatment with the three active compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350 had statistically enhanced nuclear accumulation of capsid and Fn/c values of greater than 4 (Fig 1B). In contrast, TC83_Cm infected cells treated with DMSO, Leptomycin B, or KPT-185 had Fn/c values of less than one (Fig 1C).

SINE compounds reduced released viral RNA and capsid, indicating interference with viral assembly and/or release. To test this hypothesis, the amount of viral RNA present at 4 and 8 hpi, within cells or in the extracellular supernatants, was assessed. Viral RNA detected in the extracellular supernatants would be virion-associated, while intracellular levels would represent viral RNA to be shuttled into viral protein translation, negative-sense strand RNA replication, or encapsidation into particles. The early time points were chosen to correspond to the first round of viral replication. Extracellular viral RNA was reduced upon treatment with Leptomycin B and KPT-185 at 4 and 8 hpi relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3A), with a greater relative reduction at 8 hpi. In contrast, at 4 hpi, intracellular RNA levels were approximately equal regardless of treatment, with a decrease seen by 8 hpi in both Leptomycin B and KPT-185 treated cells relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3B). Similar results were obtained at an MOI of 10 (S4 Fig). This indicates that SINE compounds likely disrupt a late step in the virus lifecycle such as viral assembly or budding.

Treatment with SINE compounds reduced VEEV replication in a dose-dependent manner. Using the luciferase assay, the EC50, the effective concentration of a compound that reduces luminescence to half that of the DMSO control, was determined. The inactive compound, KPT-301, had an EC50 value of >3 μM (Fig 4D), but the three active compounds, KPT-185 (Fig 4C), KPT-335 (Fig 4E) and KPT-350 (Fig 4F) had EC50 values that ranged from 0.62 to 0.09 μM (Table 1). The CC50 for all four compounds was greater than 10 μM and therefore 10 μM was used to calculate the selective index (SI) for each compound. All three active compounds had SI values greater than 10, indicating the compounds are good therapeutic candidates. This set of experiments demonstrated the active SINE compounds inhibited VEEV replication in a dose-dependent manner with SI values that make them good therapeutic candidates.

SINE compounds reduced VEEV-TC83, TrD, but not TC83_Cm titers. Supernatants from TC83 infected cells were collected after 16 hpi and viral titers calculated using plaque assays. The active SINE compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350, as well as Leptomycin B, were able to significantly reduce TC83 viral titers by approximately two logs (Fig 5A). As a control, cells were alternatively infected with TC83_Cm. Minimal differences in TC83_Cm titers were observed in the presence of Leptomycin B or KPT-185 (Fig 5B). Additionally, active SINE compounds reduced VEEV-TrD viral titers by approximately two logs (Fig 5C). Conversely, treatment with inactive KPT-301 yielded viral titers similar to that observed for DMSO alone (Fig 5A and 5C). Performing a similar experiment with supernatant collected at 8, 16, and 24 hpi with TC83 yielded results analogous to the 16 hpi experiments (Fig 5D), indicating the SINE compounds have an effect early on infection as well as at later time points.

SINE compounds altered capsid localization and reduced viral titers when used as a post-treatment only. All of the previous experiments involved pre- and post-treating cells with SINE compounds; however, treatment of cells after infection is more predictive of a compound’s therapeutic potential. To this end, Vero cells were infected with VEEV-TC83 for one hour, viral inocula removed, and cells washed prior to addition of media. After four hours, active SINE compounds, the inactive compound KPT-301, Leptomycin B, or DMSO was added to the media. At 8 hpi capsid localization and VEEV titers were determined. The active SINE compounds and Leptomycin B (Fig 6A and 6B and S3C Fig) all showed capsid localization patterns similar to that seen with pre- and post-treated cells (Fig 1). Post-treatment with DMSO had no effect on capsid localization, while KPT-301 (Fig 6A and 6B) had a slight but statistically significant effect on capsid localization. However, this effect was much less dramatic as compared to the active SINE compounds. Similarly, post-treatment of Vero cells infected with VEEV-TC83 significantly reduced viral titers by approximately one log (Fig 6C). Together, these results indicate that active SINE compounds may have therapeutic, in addition to prophylactic, potential.

SINE compounds reduced viral titers of other New World alphaviruses. It was hypothesized that the SINE compounds should have a similar effect on other New World alphaviruses, assuming their capsid proteins also gain access to the nucleus and interact with CRM1 to egress from the nucleus [19, 21]. Frolova’s group demonstrated that VEEV capsid contains an NLS at amino acids 64–68 and a supraNES in the region of 38–55 [17]. Using UniProt, the capsid proteins of several New World alphaviruses were aligned. The capsid proteins of VEEV, EEEV, and WEEV share critical lysine residues [17] in the positively charged NLS (Fig 8A, residues highlighted in green). A consensus NES sequence (ФxxxФxxФxФ, where Ф = L, I, F, V or M and x = any amino acid) is present in VEEV, EEEV, and WEEV capsid proteins ([17], Fig 8A). A common NES region would suggest that capsid proteins from other New World alphaviruses would interact with CRM1 in a similar manner. To test this hypothesis, SINE compounds were examined for their ability to inhibit EEEV and WEEV. A statistically significant inhibition of approximately two logs was seen with the active SINE compounds for both EEEV (Fig 8B) and WEEV (Fig 8C), while inactive KPT-301 yielded similar results to DMSO alone. This suggests that the active SINE compounds are likely acting on a common mechanism among the New World alphaviruses.
467.Rationale: The passage provides multiple pieces of evidence that support the conclusion that SINE compounds reduce viral output by interfering with viral assembly and/or budding. The evidence includes the alteration of capsid localization to the nucleus, the reduction of viral RNA in the extracellular supernatants, the dose-dependent reduction of viral replication, the reduction of viral titers in plaque assays, and the similar effects observed with other New World alphaviruses. These findings suggest that the SINE compounds sequester capsid in the nucleus, which disrupts the normal process of viral assembly and release, leading to a reduction in viral output. The passage does not provide direct evidence for the other options, making them incorrect based on the information given.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers by blocking the nuclear import of viral capsid, thereby preventing its interaction with host transcription factors.
Evidence: Chemically inhibiting the host’s nuclear import and export proteins alters VEEV capsid localization [18]. Using the same rationale, novel CRM1 inhibitors were tested for their ability to alter capsid localization. Four SINE compounds (S1A Fig) were first screened for toxicity in two separate cell lines, treated for 24 hours, and determined to be non-toxic at 2.5 μM (S2 Fig, [39, 40]). Next, Vero cells treated with non-toxic concentrations of active CRM1 inhibitors (KPT-185, KPT-335, KPT-350), inactive KPT-301, or DMSO were infected with VEEV-TC83, and capsid localization assessed at 16 hpi by confocal microscopy. As seen previously [17, 18], VEEV capsid localized to both the cytoplasm and nucleus in DMSO-treated cells. A similar phenotype was seen upon treatment with the inactive compound, KPT-301. The three active compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350, confined capsid to the nucleus (Fig 1A and S3A Fig), yielding a localization pattern similar to that seen with treatment by the well-documented CRM1 inhibitor, Leptomycin B [18]. As a control, TC83 virus with a mutated capsid that is defective in nuclear import (TC83_Cm; [38]) was analyzed. TC83_Cm infected cells treated with DMSO, Leptomycin B, or KPT-185 all displayed capsid cytoplasmic staining (Fig 1A). To quantitate capsid localization, the nuclear to cytoplasmic fluorescence ratio (Fn/c) was measured as described previously ([18] and S1B Fig). An Fn/c value greater than one denotes predominately nuclear fluorescence, whereas a score below one indicates predominantly cytoplasmic fluorescence. Treatment with the three active compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350 had statistically enhanced nuclear accumulation of capsid and Fn/c values of greater than 4 (Fig 1B). In contrast, TC83_Cm infected cells treated with DMSO, Leptomycin B, or KPT-185 had Fn/c values of less than one (Fig 1C).

SINE compounds reduced released viral RNA and capsid, indicating interference with viral assembly and/or release. To test this hypothesis, the amount of viral RNA present at 4 and 8 hpi, within cells or in the extracellular supernatants, was assessed. Viral RNA detected in the extracellular supernatants would be virion-associated, while intracellular levels would represent viral RNA to be shuttled into viral protein translation, negative-sense strand RNA replication, or encapsidation into particles. The early time points were chosen to correspond to the first round of viral replication. Extracellular viral RNA was reduced upon treatment with Leptomycin B and KPT-185 at 4 and 8 hpi relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3A), with a greater relative reduction at 8 hpi. In contrast, at 4 hpi, intracellular RNA levels were approximately equal regardless of treatment, with a decrease seen by 8 hpi in both Leptomycin B and KPT-185 treated cells relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3B). Similar results were obtained at an MOI of 10 (S4 Fig). This indicates that SINE compounds likely disrupt a late step in the virus lifecycle such as viral assembly or budding.

Treatment with SINE compounds reduced VEEV replication in a dose-dependent manner. Using the luciferase assay, the EC50, the effective concentration of a compound that reduces luminescence to half that of the DMSO control, was determined. The inactive compound, KPT-301, had an EC50 value of >3 μM (Fig 4D), but the three active compounds, KPT-185 (Fig 4C), KPT-335 (Fig 4E) and KPT-350 (Fig 4F) had EC50 values that ranged from 0.62 to 0.09 μM (Table 1). The CC50 for all four compounds was greater than 10 μM and therefore 10 μM was used to calculate the selective index (SI) for each compound. All three active compounds had SI values greater than 10, indicating the compounds are good therapeutic candidates. This set of experiments demonstrated the active SINE compounds inhibited VEEV replication in a dose-dependent manner with SI values that make them good therapeutic candidates.

SINE compounds reduced VEEV-TC83, TrD, but not TC83_Cm titers. Supernatants from TC83 infected cells were collected after 16 hpi and viral titers calculated using plaque assays. The active SINE compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350, as well as Leptomycin B, were able to significantly reduce TC83 viral titers by approximately two logs (Fig 5A). As a control, cells were alternatively infected with TC83_Cm. Minimal differences in TC83_Cm titers were observed in the presence of Leptomylin B or KPT-185 (Fig 5B). Additionally, active SINE compounds reduced VEEV-TrD viral titers by approximately two logs (Fig 5C). Conversely, treatment with inactive KPT-301 yielded viral titers similar to that observed for DMSO alone (Fig 5A and 5C). Performing a similar experiment with supernatant collected at 8, 16, and 24 hpi with TC83 yielded results analogous to the 16 hpi experiments (Fig 5D), indicating the SINE compounds have an effect early on infection as well as at later time points.

SINE compounds altered capsid localization and reduced viral titers when used as a post-treatment only. All of the previous experiments involved pre- and post-treating cells with SINE compounds; however, treatment of cells after infection is more predictive of a compound’s therapeutic potential. To this end, Vero cells were infected with VEEV-TC83 for one hour, viral inocula removed, and cells washed prior to addition of media. After four hours, active SINE compounds, the inactive compound KPT-301, Leptomycin B, or DMSO was added to the media. At 8 hpi capsid localization and VEEV titers were determined. The active SINE compounds and Leptomycin B (Fig 6A and 6B and S3C Fig) all showed capsid localization patterns similar to that seen with pre- and post-treated cells (Fig 1). Post-treatment with DMSO had no effect on capsid localization, while KPT-301 (Fig 6A and 6B) had a slight but statistically significant effect on capsid localization. However, this effect was much less dramatic as compared to the active SINE compounds. Similarly, post-treatment of Vero cells infected with VEEV-TC83 significantly reduced viral titers by approximately one log (Fig 6C). Together, these results indicate that active SINE compounds may have therapeutic, in addition to prophylactic, potential.

SINE compounds reduced viral titers of other New World alphaviruses. It was hypothesized that the SINE compounds should have a similar effect on other New World alphaviruses, assuming their capsid proteins also gain access to the nucleus and interact with CRM1 to egress from the nucleus [19, 21]. Frolova’s group demonstrated that VEEV capsid contains an NLS at amino acids 64–68 and a supraNES in the region of 38–55 [17]. Using UniProt, the capsid proteins of several New World alphaviruses were aligned. The capsid proteins of VEEV, EEEV, and WEEV share critical lysine residues [17] in the positively charged NLS (Fig 8A, residues highlighted in green). A consensus NES sequence (ФxxxФxxФxФ, where Ф = L, I, F, V or M and x = any amino acid) is present in VEEV, EEEV, and WEEV capsid proteins ([17], Fig 8A). A common NES region would suggest that capsid proteins from other New World alphaviruses would interact with CRM1 in a similar manner. To test this hypothesis, SINE compounds were examined for their ability to inhibit EEEV and WEEV. A statistically significant inhibition of approximately two logs was seen with the active SINE compounds for both EEEV (Fig 8B) and WEEV (Fig 8C), while inactive KPT-301 yielded similar results to DMSO alone. This suggests that the active SINE compounds are likely acting on a common mechanism among the New World alphaviruses.
467.Rationale: While the passage does discuss the nuclear localization of capsid and the potential for interaction with host transcription factors, the primary evidence provided supports the conclusion that the SINE compounds reduce viral output by interfering with viral assembly and/or budding, not by blocking nuclear import of the capsid. The passage does not provide direct evidence that the SINE compounds are acting by preventing interaction with host transcription factors, making this option incorrect based on the information given.

468.Option: The presence of mGMCSF fusion increases the immune response to the LdPxn1 antigen primarily due to its role as a secretory module that enhances antigen delivery to professional antigen-presenting cells.
Evidence: GMCSF has been used as an important adjuvant in infectious disease and cancer vaccine candidates.
GMCSF has been used in the form of recombinant protein or plasmid DNA as an adjuvant in candidate vaccines for malaria [17], HIV [18], mycobacteria [19], Leishmania [20], as well as cancer [21].

A GMCSF-containing therapeutic vaccine, Sipuleucel-T (Provenge, Dendreon), has been approved by US food and drug administration (FDA) for the treatment of asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic prostate cancer in men [21].
GMCSF possesses a 17 amino acid leader sequence that mediates secretion of the protein [22].
468.Rationale: The passage indicates that GMCSF has a role as an adjuvant and a secretory module, which suggests it enhances the immune response by improving antigen delivery to professional antigen-presenting cells. 470.Index: Option: The detection of a single positive case in a child from a previously treated area suggests that the mass drug administration program in Recife was ineffective in interrupting transmission.
470.Option: The detection of a single positive case in a child from a previously treated area suggests that the mass drug administration program in Recife was ineffective in interrupting transmission.
Evidence: Only one child in the municipality of Camaragibe was positive for the presence of circulating filarial antigen (Table 2). This child was a six-year-old girl from the Nova Descoberta district of the municipality of Recife (a post-MDA area). The child was born in Recife and had moved to Camaragibe with her family in 2014. She was amicrofilaremic, and ultrasound examination did not reveal any adult worm nests in the cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphatic chains. Investigations of parasite, antigens, and adult worms in other members of her family (parents and three siblings) were negative. However, her parents had undergone three rounds of MDA while living in Recife.
470.Rationale: The detection of a single positive case in a child from a previously treated area does not necessarily suggest that the mass drug administration program was ineffective. The child was born in Recife, where MDA was conducted, and moved to Camaragibe with her family. The fact that her parents had undergone MDA in Recife but the child tested positive could be due to various factors, such as migration from an endemic area, and does not directly imply ineffectiveness of the MDA program. The passage does not provide enough information to make a definitive conclusion about the effectiveness of the MDA program based on this single case.

470.Index: Option: The lack of physical and administrative barriers between municipalities means that lymphatic filariasis can be eradicated across the entire Metropolitan Region of Recife through coordinated mass drug administration campaigns.
470.Option: The lack of physical and administrative barriers between municipalities means that lymphatic filariasis can be eradicated across the entire Metropolitan Region of Recife through coordinated mass drug administration campaigns.
Evidence: Nine other municipalities (Abreu e Lima, Cabo de Santo Agostinho, Camaragibe, Igarassu, Ilha de Itamaracá, Ipojuca, Itapissuma, Moreno, and São Lourenço da Mata) are part of the Metropolitan Region of Recife and are areas that adjoin the endemic sites [10].
470.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about the presence or absence of physical and administrative barriers between municipalities. It only states that these municipalities adjoin the endemic sites. Therefore, this option cannot be derived from the passage.

470.Index: Option: The historical presence of microfilaremia and vector infection in the nine municipalities indicates that mass drug administration should be reinstated in these areas to prevent further spread of lymphatic filariasis.
470.Option: The historical presence of microfilaremia and vector infection in the nine municipalities indicates that mass drug administration should be reinstated in these areas to prevent further spread of lymphatic filariasis.
Evidence: The review of the literature identified eight epidemiological surveys performed during the study period. Six of them used thick drop tests to identify microfilaria in the blood, one used the thick drop test and investigated filarial larvae in mosquitoes, and one investigated antigens using ICTs. There was no record of any survey in the municipality of Itapissuma. Fig 2 shows that in the remaining eight municipalities, there were cases of microfilaremia, corresponding for the prevalence of <1% [11–15]. The vector infection rate was 1.1% in a single survey conducted in the municipality of São Lourenço da Mata in 1967 [12]. In 2015, an investigation of antigens conducted in the municipality of Ipojuca did not identify any cases of LF [16]. In all studies, all microfilaremia cases were treated with DEC (6 mg/kg/12 days).

Consequently, surveillance and verification of the interruption of transmission started with molecular xenomonitoring (identification of parasite DNA in vector mosquitoes) and thick drop tests in two endemic municipalities of the Metropolitan region of Recife after three TASs were stopped.
470.Rationale: The historical presence of microfilaremia and vector infection in the nine municipalities suggests that there is still a risk of transmission of lymphatic filariasis in these areas. The passage indicates that surveillance and verification of the interruption of transmission started after the cessation of MDA, implying that MDA may have been necessary to control the disease. Therefore, the historical presence of the disease in these areas supports the need for MDA to prevent further spread.

470.Index: Option: The use of immunochromatographic card tests (ICTs) in schoolchildren is a reliable method for confirming the elimination of lymphatic filariasis in areas with uncertain endemicity, as demonstrated by the absence of positive cases in the nine municipalities studied.
470.Option: The use of immunochromatographic card tests (ICTs) in schoolchildren is a reliable method for confirming the elimination of lymphatic filariasis in areas with uncertain endemicity, as demonstrated by the absence of positive cases in the nine municipalities studied.
Evidence: The review of the literature identified eight epidemiological surveys performed during the study period. Six of them used thick drop tests to identify microfilaria in the blood, one used the thick drop test and investigated filarial larvae in mosquitoes, and one investigated antigens using ICTs. There was no record of any survey in the municipality of Itapissuma. Fig 2 shows that in the remaining eight municipalities, there were cases of microfilaremia, corresponding for the prevalence of <1% [11–15]. The vector infection rate was 1.1% in a single survey conducted in the municipality of São Lourenço da Mata in 1967 [12]. In 2015, an investigation of antigens conducted in the municipality of Ipojuca did not identify any cases of LF [16]. In all studies, all microfilaremia cases were treated with DEC (6 mg/kg/12 days).

Consequently, surveillance and verification of the interruption of transmission started with molecular xenomonitoring (identification of parasite DNA in vector mosquitoes) and thick drop tests in two endemic municipalities of the Metropolitan region of Recife after three TASs were stopped.
470.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the claim that the use of ICTs is a reliable method for confirming the elimination of lymphatic filariasis. Therefore, this option cannot be derived from the passage.

470.Index: Option: Even in areas with low endemicity that have stopped mass drug administration, ongoing surveillance is necessary to prevent the risk of disease recrudescence due to migration and the presence of vector breeding sites.
470.Option: Even in areas with low endemicity that have stopped mass drug administration, ongoing surveillance is necessary to prevent the risk of disease recrudescence due to migration and the presence of vector breeding sites.
Evidence: Consequently, surveillance and verification of the interruption of transmission started with molecular xenomonitoring (identification of parasite DNA in vector mosquitoes) and thick drop tests in two endemic municipalities of the Metropolitan region of Recife after three TASs were stopped.

A country is validated for elimination of LF as a public health problem if 1) it has demonstrated reduction in the prevalence of infection in endemic areas below a target threshold at which further transmission is considered unlikely even in the absence of MDA and 2) it ensures the availability of the minimum package of care for lymphoedema and hydrocele to alleviate suffering caused by the disease [19,20].
470.Rationale: The passage indicates that surveillance and verification of the interruption of transmission started after the cessation of MDA, suggesting that ongoing surveillance is necessary to prevent the risk of disease recrudescence. The passage also mentions that a country is validated for elimination of LF as a public health problem if it has demonstrated a reduction in the prevalence of infection in endemic areas below a target threshold, even in the absence of MDA. This implies that ongoing surveillance is necessary to prevent the risk of disease recrudescence, especially in areas with low endemicity.

471.Option: Despite high levels of knowledge about schistosomiasis transmission, schoolchildren in Mbita Sub-county continue to engage in high-risk behaviors due to the lack of safe water sources and sanitation infrastructure, indicating a critical gap between knowledge and practice.
Evidence: More than 75% of pupils in both 2015 and 2016 answered that blood in urine and blood in stool were main signs of schistosomiasis.

In terms of prevention, “Using protective boots and gloves when we contact with water from lake”, “avoiding contact with contaminated water” and “using toilet for urination/defecation” were the top three major answers for both years.
Pupils cited schoolteachers (more than 90%) or hospital/health centre (about 80%) as their main sources of information.
Attitude toward infection opportunity during daily life and activities at the lake were statistically different (p < 0.001 in 2016 by χ2 test).
In terms of practice, more than 70%, 80%, and 70% of pupils bathed, fetched water and washed clothes/utensils, respectively at Lake Victoria every day or sometimes in both years.
When asked about sanitation habits, about 85% of the pupils reported that they regularly use toilet while in school, but the figure statistically decreased to about 67% while at home in both years.
471.Rationale: The passage indicates that there is a high level of knowledge among schoolchildren about the transmission of schistosomiasis, as evidenced by their correct answers regarding transmission modes and symptoms. However, despite this knowledge, the children continue to engage in high-risk behaviors such as bathing, fetching water, and washing clothes in Lake Victoria, which is a source of schistosomiasis infection. This suggests a gap between their knowledge and their practices, likely due to the lack of safe water sources and sanitation infrastructure. The statistics showing the difference in toilet use at school versus at home further support the idea that there is a critical gap between what the children know and what they practice, especially in their home environments.

471.Option: The high rate of toilet use at school compared to at home suggests that community-level sanitation infrastructure is more developed than household-level infrastructure in Mbita Sub-county.
Evidence: When asked about sanitation habits, about 85% of the pupils reported that they regularly use toilet while in school, but the figure statistically decreased to about 67% while at home in both years.
471.Rationale: The passage actually suggests the opposite; the high rate of toilet use at school compared to at home indicates that the sanitation infrastructure at the school level is more developed than at the household level. This is not a suggestion that community-level infrastructure is more developed than household-level infrastructure, but rather that schools have better facilities than homes.

471.Option: The contradiction between school water-fetching policies and health education messages indicates that school administrators are unaware of the risks of schistosomiasis transmission through lake water.
Evidence: Pupils cited schoolteachers (more than 90%) or hospital/health centre (about 80%) as their main sources of information.
471.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence to support the claim that school administrators are unaware of the risks. Instead, it suggests that the pupils' high-risk behaviors may be due to a lack of safe water sources and sanitation infrastructure, not necessarily a lack of awareness among school administrators. The passage also does not directly address the policies of school administrators regarding water-fetching.

471.Option: The use of school-based health education through science textbooks has been ineffective in improving students' knowledge about schistosomiasis, as evidenced by the high prevalence of misconceptions about transmission and symptoms.
Evidence: Pupils in class six learn about schistosomiasis in the science subject as “bilharzia”–a water-borne disease. Two kinds of textbooks published by different publishers were available in the study area, and their use depended on preference of the school.
However, one of the textbooks wrongly described that drinking contaminated water is a mode of transmission, and the other missed the symptoms of intestinal schistosomiasis.
471.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that the use of school-based health education has been ineffective. It only states that one of the textbooks contained a misconception about transmission. The passage does not provide information on the overall effectiveness of the health education or the prevalence of misconceptions among the students.

472.Option: The presence of a frame-shift mutation in ML0825c, encoding a zinc-sensing transcriptional regulator, suggests that the recurrent strain in patient 2188 had evolved increased virulence due to functional impairment of the SmtB protein.
Evidence: Analysis of 2188–2007 and 2188–2014 revealed identical genome sequences (Fig 1). Curiously, both strains belong to a new SNP subtype intermediate between subtypes 4N and 4O. The only difference between the two genomes was found in the (GTA)9 VNTR locus (S5 Table), which harbored 11 repeats in 2188–2007 and 12 repeats in 2188–2014.

Another frame-shift arising from a dinucleotide insertion was found in ML0825c, the ortholog of rv2358 in M. tuberculosis that codes for the protein SmtB, a zinc-sensing transcriptional regulator and member of the AsrR/SmtB family.

The C-terminal part of SmtB is essential for the protein dimerization, zinc binding and DNA recognition. Furthermore, a specific histidine residue (H138 in ML0825c and H117 in Synechococcus StmB) is important for the allosteric coupling of the zinc and DNA binding sites in the protein.
Modeling of M. leprae StmB in silico (S2 Fig) showed that the frame-shift leads to loss of H138 and should thus impair protein function.
472.Rationale: The passage describes a frame-shift mutation in the ML0825c gene, which encodes a zinc-sensing transcriptional regulator, and suggests that this mutation could impair the function of the SmtB protein. However, the passage does not provide evidence that this mutation led to increased virulence or that the recurrent strain in patient 2188 had evolved increased virulence. The mutation's impact on protein function is discussed, but there is no direct link made to increased virulence or the nature of the clinical relapses. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

472.Option: The extended observation period allowed identification of relapses/reinfection, and whole-genome sequencing enabled differentiation between relapse and reinfection in a field setting with high resolution.
Evidence: In this study, we investigated three recurrent cases of leprosy from the U-MDT/CT-BR trial to determine whether recurrence was due to drug resistance, bacterial persistence or to reinfection.

To achieve this, we compared whole genome sequencing analysis of M. leprae collected from skin lesions at the initial diagnosis and during the recurrence of the disease and correlated the sequence data with the clinical, microbiologic and serologic findings.

The recurrent strain 1126–2011 was clearly distinct from the primary strain 1126–2007, and differed in 44 SNPs, 4 InDels and 6 VNTR loci (Fig 1 and S5 Table). Furthermore, 1126–2007 and 1126–2011 share no SNPs that might indicate close relatedness or direct ancestry.

Analysis of 2188–2007 and 2188–2014 revealed identical genome sequences (Fig 1). Curiously, both strains belong to a new SNP subtype intermediate between subtypes 4N and 4O. The only difference between the two genomes was found in the (GTA)9 VNTR locus (S5 Table), which harbored 11 repeats in 2188–2007 and 12 repeats in 2188–2014.

Genome comparisons revealed that both strains share 28 unique variant nucleotides (S7 Table). Among them are two missense mutations in ML0411, encoding a PPE protein and in ribD (ML1340), the riboflavin biosynthesis protein. An insertion of 9 nucleotides (GGACATCTA at position 1,219,061) was found in ML1052, a putative PucR-like transcriptional regulator, which leads to a modification of the protein. Interestingly this mutation was present at only 30% frequency in 2188–2007, while it was fixed in 2188–2014.
472.Rationale: The passage indicates that the study investigated three recurrent cases of leprosy and used whole-genome sequencing to compare the genomes of M. leprae from the initial diagnosis and during recurrence. This allowed the researchers to determine whether the recurrence was due to drug resistance, bacterial persistence, or reinfection. The extended observation period and the use of whole-genome sequencing with high resolution were key factors that enabled the accurate determination of the nature of clinical relapses. This option is correct.

472.Option: The high antibody titers to PGL-1 and LID-1 antigens during and after treatment suggest that patients remained immunologically responsive to M. leprae, which may have contributed to the recurrence of leprosy.
Evidence: In addition, the first diagnosis revealed that the three MB patients showed high IgM and IgG antibody levels to PGL-1 and LID-1 antigens, respectively (S1 Fig).
Overall, the antibody titers gradually declined but remained above the threshold for positivity for at least one of the antigens during the study period except for patient 1126.
This patient showed an antibody titer below the threshold just before the recurrence of the disease (39 months after U-MDT) and then both antibody titers increased by the time of recurrent disease.
472.Rationale: While the passage does mention high antibody titers to PGL-1 and LID-1 antigens, it does not suggest that these titers contributed to the recurrence of leprosy. Instead, it indicates that the antibody titers gradually declined but remained above the threshold for positivity for at least one of the antigens during the study period, except for one patient just before the recurrence. The passage does not establish a causal relationship between high antibody titers and the recurrence of leprosy, making this option incorrect.

472.Option: The use of a custom-synthesized oligonucleotide array to remove host DNA from libraries improved the sensitivity of whole-genome sequencing in clinical specimens, enabling reliable detection of low-abundance M. leprae strains.
Evidence: To remove host DNA from the libraries, we used a custom-synthesized oligonucleotide array (Agilent) spanning the entire M. leprae genome.
Quality of the captured and re-amplified library was assessed using the Fragment Analyzer system (Advances Analytical technologies, Inc).
The size of the captured library was 180bp and the concentration 52ng/μl.
472.Rationale: The passage explains that a custom-synthesized oligonucleotide array was used to remove host DNA from the libraries, which improved the sensitivity of whole-genome sequencing in clinical specimens. This enabled the reliable detection of low-abundance M. leprae strains, which is a methodological factor that contributed to the study's ability to accurately determine the nature of clinical relapses. This option is correct.

473.Option: The high sensitivity of the CareStart G6PD test ensures that all patients with true G6PD deficiency are correctly identified, which supports the safe and effective use of standard daily primaquine in the majority of treated patients.
Evidence: Diagnostic performance of the test was calculated comparing the quantitative point-of-care test (cut-off for deficiency: < 4.0 IU/gHb, as per manufacturer’s instructions).
During implementation (February 2019 to early January 2020), 72 G6PD deficient and 267 G6PD normal cases were detected using the CareStart RDT.
Diagnostic parameters showed 100% sensitivity (4/4; 95%CI 39.8–100.0) and 68.1% specificity (62/91; 95%CI 57.5–77.5), with a prevalence of 4.2% (95%CI 1.2–10.4).
Negative and positive predictive values were 100% (95%CI 94.2–100.0) and 12.1% (95%CI 3.4–28.2), respectively.
473.Rationale: While the test does have 100% sensitivity, the specificity is only 68.1%, which means that a significant number of patients without G6PD deficiency (false positives) are also being identified as deficient. This would not support the safe and effective use of standard daily primaquine in the majority of treated patients, as many would be incorrectly classified as unable to receive the treatment. The high number of false deficient results (29 out of 33) indicates that the test's poor specificity is a concern.

473.Option: The implementation of the CareStart G6PD test in the Brazilian Amazon has led to improved patient adherence to weekly primaquine regimens due to increased trust in the diagnostic process.
Evidence: Most patients (80.5%) understood that the reason for the test was to check resistance to the medication or any type of “allergy”.

Respondents also reported how patients were sensitized to the G6PD test. Most of the interviewees (68.3%) believed that patients had difficulties understanding the concept of the test but accepted to do it because there was a trust long established due to years of malaria work in the Amazon.
473.Rationale: The passage does not provide direct evidence that the implementation of the CareStart G6PD test has led to improved patient adherence to weekly primaquine regimens. It does indicate that patients understood the reason for the test and that there was trust in the diagnostic process, but it does not specifically link this to adherence to the regimens. Therefore, this inference cannot be made solely from the passage body.

473.Option: The lack of a control line in the CareStart G6PD test prevents the identification of invalid results, which may lead to misinterpretation of test outcomes and compromise the reliability of G6PD screening in field conditions.
Evidence: The test does not have a control line. Intermediate results cannot be distinguished clearly, and thus faint color changes were also considered deficient. An invalid result was considered when blood did not run correctly through the testing window.
During implementation (February 2019 to early January 2020), 72 G6PD deficient and 267 G6PD normal cases were detected using the CareStart RDT.
Diagnostic parameters showed 100% sensitivity (4/4; 95%CI 39.8–100.0) and 68.1% specificity (62/91; 95%CI 57.5–77.5), with a prevalence of 4.2% (95%CI 1.2–10.4).
Negative and positive predictive values were 100% (95%CI 94.2–100.0) and 12.1% (95%CI 3.4–28.2), respectively.
473.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the CareStart G6PD test does not have a control line, which can lead to misinterpretation of test outcomes, as intermediate results cannot be distinguished clearly and faint color changes are considered deficient. This can result in invalid results when blood does not run correctly through the testing window. These factors can compromise the reliability of G6PD screening in field conditions, making this inference correct.

473.Option: Although the CareStart G6PD rapid diagnostic test showed 100% sensitivity, its poor specificity led to a high number of false deficient results, resulting in many patients being incorrectly classified as unable to receive standard daily primaquine treatment.
Evidence: Diagnostic parameters showed 100% sensitivity (4/4; 95%CI 39.8–100.0) and 68.1% specificity (62/91; 95%CI 57.5–77.5), with a prevalence of 4.2% (95%CI 1.2–10.4).
Negative and positive predictive values were 100% (95%CI 94.2–100.0) and 12.1% (95%CI 3.4–28.2), respectively.
In this scenario, 29 (87%) false deficient patients out of 33 diagnosed in the RDT missed the chance of radical cure due to low specificity.
473.Rationale: The passage provides data showing that the CareStart G6PD test has 100% sensitivity but only 68.1% specificity, leading to a high number of false deficient results. This misclassification of patients as unable to receive standard daily primaquine treatment is directly supported by the evidence provided, making this inference correct.

473.Option: The use of the CareStart G6PD test in routine malaria treatment units has been shown to significantly reduce hospitalization rates related to PQ-induced hemolysis in the Brazilian Amazon.
Evidence: Although AHA can lead to severe anemia and acute kidney injury [4,5], with an estimated impact on hospitalization costs of US$ 6 million per year to the Brazilian public health budget [6], there is currently no routine G6PDd screening performed in vivax malaria patients prior to treatment in Brazil, where all patients are given standard short-course PQ treatment (0.5 mg/kg/day for 7 days) [7].
In most of these cases, hospitalization lasts only a few days.

It only mentions that hospitalization typically lasts only a few days, but it does not establish a direct link between the use of the test and a reduction in hospitalization rates. Therefore, this inference cannot be made solely from the passage body.

Evidence: Univariate Cox regression revealed that age (≥60 years), bleeding tendency (HR = 2.385, 95%CI: 1.38–4.123, P = 0.002), respiratory symptom (HR = 1.833, 95%CI: 1.028–3.27, P = 0.04), PCT (HR = 4.621, 95%CI: 2.761–7.734, P = 0.012) and FPG (HR = 8.778, 95% CI: 4.931–15.625, P<0.0001) were risk factors for in-hospital death among patients with SFTS without preexisting diabetes.

After adjusting for some clinical variables, age (≥60 years), tremor, and high PCT and FPG levels (≥11.1 mmol/L) were independently associated with in-hospital death among these patients (Fig 4A). As age was also an independent risk factor for in-hospital death in these patients, we conducted a subgroup analysis based on age. Higher FPG levels on admission were associated with an increased in-hospital mortality rate in patients aged <60 years (P = 0.0067) and ≥60 years (P<0.0001, Fig 3B and 3C). In summary, high FPG levels on admission were a potent risk factor for in-hospital mortality among individuals with SFTS without preexisting diabetes.

481.Rationale: The passage indicates that high FPG levels were not found to be a risk factor for secondary infections in the study population, which could be due to the routine use of antibiotics. 482.Option: Since Bartonella was detected in mites from both M. musculus and M. booduga, and both species are synanthropic, mites in human habitations are likely to be the main source of zoonotic Bartonella infections in this region.
Evidence: Bartonella was present in 52.48% of the small mammals tested (Table 1). Among the most common four species, R. satarae had the highest prevalence of Bartonella at 85.07% (n = 67) (Fig 2). Synanthropic species M. musculus had a prevalence of 58.33% (n = 24), followed by M. booduga at 2.85% (n = 35), a species abundant in grasslands but often present in forests and human habitations. All the R. rattus, another well-recognized synanthrope, screened were PCR-negative for Bartonella. The only individual of F. tristriatus caught was positive while one of the two S. murinus individuals was positive. Screening results obtained from dried blood spots were consistent with the spleen samples (Table 2). Of the seven mite pools screened, one pool from M. musculus (n = 3) and another one from M. booduga (n = 1) was positive. Other mite pools from R. satarae, G. ellioti, and P. lasiurus were negative. A list of all individual small mammals used in this study and Bartonella screening results for spleen and dried blood spots are provided in S1 File.
482.Rationale: The passage indicates that Bartonella was detected in mites from M. musculus and M. booduga, which are synanthropic species. However, it also states that R. satarae had the highest prevalence of Bartonella and that R. rattus, another synanthropic species, was PCR-negative for Bartonella. Additionally, only one individual of F. tristriatus and one of S. murinus were positive for Bartonella. This suggests that mites in human habitations are not the main source of zoonotic Bartonella infections in this region, as the highest prevalence was found in R. satarae, which is not a synanthropic species. Therefore, the option is incorrect.

482.Option: The haplotype accumulation curve suggests that mites in this ecosystem are likely to carry a higher diversity of Bartonella haplotypes than their host species, indicating a greater role in pathogen diversification.
Evidence: Of the 74 PCR-positives, 65 rpoB sequences (63 sequences from small mammals and two sequences from pooled mite samples) met quality thresholds and were used to construct the haplotype network and calculate haplotype diversity. Analysis of 657 bp of these rpoB sequences revealed 13 haplotypes, and haplotype diversity of 0.9 (Fig 3A). All of the haplotypes were host-specific, and within two host species, multiple haplotypes were observed. Five haplotypes each were observed in R. satarae (with two distinct lineages RS1 and RS2) and synanthropic species M. musculus (with another two distinct lineages MM1 and MM2). Both of these species had a high prevalence among the common species found in the landscape. Among the five haplotypes identified from M. musculus, four were closely related, and three of them (satellite haplotypes) originated from a central haplotype. The sequence from the pooled mites from M. musculus also belonged to this central haplotype. The results of the haplotype network were used to create a haplotype accumulation curve (Fig 3B). The shape of the curve indicates that there is a significant turnover of Bartonella haplotypes in the small mammals sampled. The extrapolated region of the haplotype accumulation curve revealed three more possible haplotypes, with the curve reaching the asymptote at around 160 positive individuals. Additional sampling of 100 positive individuals is estimated to capture all the haplotypes present in the small mammals. This indicates that we captured the majority (81%) of the haplotype diversity at the sampling sites.
482.Rationale: The passage provides information about the haplotype diversity of Bartonella in small mammals and mites, but it does not directly compare the diversity of haplotypes between mites and their host species. The haplotype accumulation curve indicates that the majority of haplotypes were captured in the sampled small mammals, but it does not provide evidence that mites carry a higher diversity of Bartonella haplotypes than their host species. Therefore, the option is incorrect.

483.Option: The inflammatory environment in acute dengue infection contributes to endothelial glycocalyx dysfunction, as evidenced by strong positive correlations between plasma syndecan-1 concentrations and multiple inflammatory proteins.
Evidence: Participants with ascites and/or pleural effusion during hospitalization had a trend for a higher ESM1 value (S5A Fig). In contrast, plasma concentrations of syndecan-1 (SDC1), a surrogate marker of endothelial glycocalyx degradation [59], correlated positively with a large number of inflammatory proteins (Fig 4) and fluid accumulation during hospitalization (S5B Fig).

The strongest associations were observed for HGF (rs = 0.74; FDR 1.18x10-6) and IL18R1 (rs = 0.71; FDR 6.59x10-6). The heparanase/SDC1 axis is known to regulate HGF expression and function [60, 61]. SDC1 concentrations were also positively correlated with a number of other endothelium-related proteins, including glycocalyx-associated proteoglycans (agrin [AGRN] and ESM1), CRIM1, and osteoprotegerin/TNFRSF11B).
The downregulation of TNFSF11/RANKL and upregulation of osteoprotegerin suggest that the RANK-RANKL-OPG axis is a key regulator of immune activation in dengue, independent of vascular integrity.
483.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of a positive correlation between plasma syndecan-1 concentrations and multiple inflammatory proteins, which is indicative of endothelial glycocalyx degradation. This suggests that the inflammatory environment in acute dengue infection contributes to the dysfunction of the endothelial glycocalyx, a key component of vascular integrity. The evidence directly supports the inference that systemic inflammation is associated with vascular integrity issues in the context of dengue infection.

483.Option: The lack of significant associations between inflammatory proteins and clinical signs of dengue severity, such as hematocrit and fluid accumulation, indicates that systemic inflammation does not play a role in disease progression.
Evidence: Overall, there were no differences in levels of inflammation-related proteins between patients classified as DF and those with DHF (S4A and S4B Fig).
In our cohort, especially age was associated with different inflammatory markers including TNF, IL1RN, chemokines and urokinase receptor (PLAUR) (Fig 4).

Participants classified as having a probable secondary dengue infection had higher concentrations of different molecules involved in the host immune response, including lymphocyte function (CEACAM21, FOXO1, MZB1, PARP1, PPP1R9B, PSIP1, SH2D1A), glucocorticoid hormone regulation (CRHBP) and megakaryocyte maturation (MPIG6B). In contrast, no differences were observed in concentrations of pro-inflammatory cytokines, interferon-inducible effector proteins or granzymes.
483.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim that there is a lack of significant associations between inflammatory proteins and clinical signs of dengue severity. It actually provides evidence of associations between certain inflammatory proteins and clinical parameters, such as the trend for higher ESM1 values in participants with ascites and/or pleural effusion. Therefore, the inference that systemic inflammation does not play a role in disease progression is incorrect based on the passage body.

483.Option: The upregulation of interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs) such as GBP2 and TRIM21 suggests that the innate immune response is primarily responsible for controlling viral replication in acute dengue infection.
Evidence: The most prominently upregulated proteins were granzyme B (GZMB) and a number of interferon (IFN)-inducible effector proteins, including the intracellular antibody receptor tripartite motif-containing protein 21 (TRIM21), IFN-induced Protein 10 (IP-10; also known as chemokine C-X-C motif ligand 10 [CXCL10]), guanylate-binding protein 2 (GBP2), sterile alpha motif domain-containing 9-like (SAMDL9) and sialic acid binding immunoglobulin-like lectin 1 (SIGLEC1), also known as sialoadhesin and CD169 (Figs 1C and 2).

The cytokines and cytokine receptors that were most markedly upregulated were interleukin (IL)-10, IL-33, IFN-gamma (IFNG), CXCL8 (also known as IL-8), IL-18 receptor-1 (IL18R1) and IL-1 receptor antagonist (IL1RN). Less markedly upregulated (Log2FC 0.55–1.37) cytokines and cytokine receptors were IL-6, tumor necrosis factor (TNF), IL-12 receptor beta 1 subunit (IL12RB1), IFN lambda receptor 1 (IFNLR1), IL-18, IL-17C and lymphotoxin alpha (LTA).
483.Rationale: While the passage does mention the upregulation of interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs) such as GBP2 and TRIM21, it does not provide evidence that the innate immune response is primarily responsible for controlling viral replication. The passage does not make a direct causal inference about the role of ISGs in viral replication control, so this option cannot be derived from the passage body.

Evidence: The strongest associations were observed for HGF (rs = 0.74; FDR 1.18x10-6) and IL18R1 (rs = 0.71; FDR 6.59x10-6). The heparanase/SDC1 axis is known to regulate HGF expression and function [60, 61]. SDC1 concentrations were also positively correlated with a number of other endothelium-related proteins, including glycocalyx-associated proteoglycans (agrin [AGRN] and ESM1), CRIM1, and osteoprotegerin/TNFRSF11B).
The downregulation of TNFSF11/RANKL and upregulation of osteoprotegerin suggest that the RANK-RANKL-OPG axis is a key regulator of immune activation in dengue, independent of vascular integrity.

Therefore, the inference that these proteins are direct mediators of endothelial glycocalyx degradation is not supported by the passage body.

484.Option: The transition to electronic data collection and centralized storage would reduce data redundancy, improve data quality, and automate reconciliation and compilation of data, which are currently performed manually and consume significant time and resources.
Evidence: Issues related to data quality were expressed by the interviewees.
Paper-based survey forms are prone to physical damage or loss and errors in handwritten information is a reality.
Data could be inadvertently or maliciously changed, and auditing is more difficult because of the lack of version control.

Data were first collected in paper-based surveillance forms by field staff. These were electronically entered via Microsoft Excel and were stored in separate locations including CGWEP issued laptops, The Carter Center’s cloud-based storage platform, personal laptops, and email folders.
Although quality checks were regularly performed on data collected for an individual form, manual quality checks were only occasionally performed across spreadsheets.

Data were reconciled and compiled into reports on a monthly basis. Data were stored in different locations and transported using multiple methods such as email and flash drive transfer within Chad when there was no or limited internet access.
There were no standardized workflows for these processes.
The results of the IVC by each of the six themes are described below.

Plan: CGWEP collected both programmatic data and epidemiologic surveillance data. The staff reported having received adequate training in surveillance and program activities, but they faced challenges in technology adoption due to limited connectivity in the region.

Capture: Data were first collected in paper-based surveillance forms by field staff. These were electronically entered via Microsoft Excel and were stored in separate locations including CGWEP issued laptops, The Carter Center’s cloud-based storage platform, personal laptops, and email folders.

Manage: Data were reconciled and compiled into reports on a monthly basis. Data were stored in different locations and transported using multiple methods such as email and flash drive transfer within Chad when there was no or limited internet access.

Analyze: Monthly reports were compiled and reported to partner agencies. Ad hoc analyses and reports were created in separate tabs on Microsoft Excel spreadsheets. More detailed and longitudinal analyses for reports and other publications were regularly conducted by program epidemiologists using statistical software.
Use: Data were fundamental for informing strategies for case containment, health promotion, and provisioning safe drinking water.

Evaluate: Accurate, timely data are required for continued program success. Several processes were identified that could improve data collection and management, including more centralized storage practices and streamlining of workflows systems.
484.Rationale: The passage discusses the inefficiencies and challenges of the current paper-based data collection system, including the potential for data redundancy, errors, and the manual effort required for data reconciliation and compilation. It also describes the transition to electronic data collection and the use of Microsoft Excel for data entry and storage. The option correctly identifies that an integrated electronic system would address these issues by reducing redundancy, improving data quality, and automating data reconciliation and compilation, which are currently performed manually.

499.Option: The Nb/mAb 'hybrid' sandwich technology overcomes a key technical barrier to the development of lateral flow assays for trypanosomiasis by enabling effective labeling of the detection reagent with gold nanoparticles.
Evidence: The passage states: 'The deployment of Ag detection POCT has facilitated the control of malaria [17] and COVID-19 [18] among other infectious diseases. However, past efforts to develop similar mAb-based Ag detection tests for trypanosomiasis have been futile [19]. The mAb-based Ag detection test prototype for trypanosomiasis suffers from low sensitivity, mostly attributed to low Ag loads in specimens resulting from sequestration of Ag in immune complex, and an inherently low pathogen loads [20]. The first description of Nb technology 30 years ago by Hamers-Casterman et al [21] reinvigorated discovery research, targeting the technology, for the development of Ag detection tests for AAT [22,23]. Typically, Nbs possess a relatively extended complementarity determining region three (CDR3), compared to conventional Abs [24], thus allowing their preferential bindings to cryptic epitopes [25,26]. The fact that Nbs can bind Ag-Ab immune complexes through a unique epitope recognition, motivated its exploration as a tool of choice for outwitting low sensitivity of Ag-detection tests, caused by parasite-induced host Abs interference.'

The passage also states: 'The potential for translation of Nb474 and Gold-labelled IgMA82 (IgM8A2) 'hybrid' sandwich technology into a LFA device was demonstrated in a dot-blot assay. Specifically, IgM8A2 was labelled with Gold 20nm using a Gold Conjugation Kit (abcam, Cat. No. ab188215) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Specifically, IgM8A2 was dialysed in 2-(N-Morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid hydrate, 4-Morpholineethanesulfonic acid (MES hydrate) buffer at pH 7.4. The dialysed mAb was later diluted to 0.25 mg/ml in an Ab diluent provided by the kit. Thereafter, an aliquot (12 μl) of the diluted mAb was mixed with the kit’s Reaction Buffer (42 μl). The mixture (45 μl) was dispensed into a vial of lyophilized Gold 20 nm followed by reconstitution and then incubation (15 mins, 22°C). A Quencher solution, provided by the kit, was added (5μl) into the mixture. To wash off unlabelled mAb, a solution of the Quencher diluted (1/10) in distilled water was added to the mixture followed by centrifugation (9000 x g, 22°C). The supernatant was carefully drawn and the resultant pellet was resuspended in a fresh diluted Quencher (50 μl). Thereafter, the binding of IgM8A2-G to test proteins including TcoALD, T. congolense lysate (TcoLys), LmALD, and TevENO was examined. The test proteins were either blotted direct on a nitrocellulose membrane (S4 Table) or anchored onto a nitrocellulose membrane by a blotted Nb474H capture reagent (S5 Table). To examine IgM8A2-G binding to test or control proteins blotted direct on the nitrocellulose membrane, each of the test samples was spotted (30 μg/spot) into a 'well' encircled by an adhesive label (Chengu 400 Pieces Self-Adhesive Reinforcement Labels). When the sample spots were damp-dried, the membrane was blocked by immersing into a 5% milk solution and incubated with gentle rocking for an overnight at 22°C. The blocking buffer was rinsed thrice with PBS-T and IgM8A2-G solution was spotted (10 μl/spot) on the 'wells' previously blotted with the samples. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 45 mins at 22°C. The membrane was washed thrice with PBS-T with each wash lasting for 30 mins. Afterward, the membrane was blot-dried and the photograph was acquired by a camera.'

The passage provides evidence that the Nb/mAb 'hybrid' sandwich technology overcomes a key technical barrier to the development of lateral flow assays for trypanosomiasis by enabling effective labeling of the detection reagent with gold nanoparticles, which is a key technical advancement in the context of developing point-of-care diagnostics for trypanosomiasis.
499.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the Nb/mAb 'hybrid' sandwich technology overcomes a key technical barrier to the development of lateral flow assays for trypanosomiasis by enabling effective labeling of the detection reagent with gold nanoparticles. This is a key technical advancement in the context of developing point-of-care diagnostics for trypanosomiasis.
